76.play: O God, did Romeo’s hand shed Tybalt’s blood?

77.play: It did, it did, alas the day, it did.

78.play: O serpent heart hid with a flow’ring face!
notes: O serpent, &c.] HENLEY. So in Macbeth, I, v, 66. [Sing. MAL. So in King John, II, i, 68: ‘With ladies faces, and fierce dragons spleens.’ Again in Hen. VIII: III, i, 145: ‘You have angel’s faces, but Heaven knows your hearts.’ [Sing.

79.play: Did ever dragon keep so fair a cave?

83.play: Just opposite to what thou justly seem’st,

84.play: A damnèd saint, an honorable villain.

85.play: O nature, what hadst thou to do in hell

86.play: When thou didst bower the spirit of a fiend
notes: bower] COLL. (ed. 2). We hesitate to alter here, because ‘bower’ is very intelligible and figuratively beautiful in connection with ‘paradise;’ but the (MS.) has, rather prosaically, pour, which, however, was formerly often spelt power.

87.play: In mortal paradise of such sweet flesh?

88.play: Was ever book containing such vile matter

89.play: So fairly bound? O, that deceit should dwell

92.play: No faith, no honesty in men. All perjured,

93.play: All forsworn, all naught, all dissemblers.

94.play: Ah, where’s my man? Give me some aqua vitae.

95.play: These griefs, these woes, these sorrows make me old.

98.play: For such a wish! He was not born to shame.

99.play: Upon his brow shame is ashamed to sit,

100.play: For ’tis a throne where honor may be crowned

101.play: Sole monarch of the universal Earth.

102.play: O, what a beast was I to chide at him!

103.play: Will you speak well of him that killed your cousin?

104.play: Shall I speak ill of him that is my husband?

105.play: Ah, poor my lord, what tongue shall smooth thy name

106.play: When I, thy three-hours wife, have mangled it?

107.play: But wherefore, villain, didst thou kill my cousin?

108.play: That villain cousin would have killed my husband.

109.play: Back, foolish tears, back to your native spring;

110.play: Your tributary drops belong to woe,

111.play: Which you, mistaking, offer up to joy.

112.play: My husband lives, that Tybalt would have slain,

113.play: And Tybalt’s dead, that would have slain my husband.

114.play: All this is comfort. Wherefore weep I then?

115.play: Some word there was, worser than Tybalt’s death,

116.play: That murdered me. I would forget it fain,

117.play: But, O, it presses to my memory

118.play: Like damnèd guilty deeds to sinners’ minds:

121.play: Hath slain ten thousand Tybalts. Tybalt’s death
notes: Hath slain ten thousand] M. MASON. That is, worse than the loss of ten thousand Tybalts. [Sing. Hudds.

122.play: Was woe enough if it had ended there;

123.play: Or, if sour woe delights in fellowship
notes: sour woe, &c.] STEEV. Thus the Latin hexameter (I know not whence it comes): ‘Solamen miseris socios habuisse doloris.’ [Sta. ULR. This corresponds to our proverbial phrase, ‘Misfortune never comes alone.’ Steevens and Malone erroneously take the words in the sense of the familiar Latin verse: Solamen, &c.

124.play: And needly will be ranked with other griefs,
notes: needly] CLARKE. Sh. has here coined an excellent word, which is not given among dictionary words, but which it would be well to adopt into our language as good English.

125.play: Why followed not, when she said “Tybalt’s dead,”

126.play: “Thy father” or “thy mother,” nay, or both,

127.play: Which modern lamentation might have moved?
notes: modern] STEEV. It means trite, common. So in As You Like It, II, vii, 156: ‘Full of wise saws and modern instances.’ [Sing. Hudds. Sta. STA. That is, ordinary, well-known lamentation. So in All’s Well, II, iii, 2. DYCE. ‘Per modo tutto fuor del modern’ uso.’—Dante, Purg. xvi, 42; where Biagioli remarks: ‘Moderno, s’ usa qui in senso di ordinario.’

128.play: But with a rearward following Tybalt’s death,
notes: rear-ward] COLL. (ed. 2). Might we not read rear-ward, though the old copies are uniform. DYCE (ed. 2). ‘Perhaps Collier’s conjecture is right.’—W. N. LETTSOM.

129.play: “Romeo is banishèd.” To speak that word

130.play: Is father, mother, Tybalt, Romeo, Juliet,

131.play: All slain, all dead. “Romeo is banishèd.”

132.play: There is no end, no limit, measure, bound,

133.play: In that word’s death. No words can that woe sound.

134.play: Where is my father and my mother, nurse?

135.play: Weeping and wailing over Tybalt’s corse.

136.play: Will you go to them? I will bring you thither.

137.play: Wash they his wounds with tears? Mine shall be spent,
notes: with tears:] STA. All the modern eds. place a note of interrogation after these words, but perhaps in error. The Nurse tells Juliet her father and mother are weeping over Tybalt’s corse, and asks if she will go to them; to which Juliet replies, ‘No, let them wash his wounds with tears; mine shall be spent in wailing Romeo’s banishment.’ S. WALKER. Point with the folio—‘Wash—tears: mine,’ &c., abluant.

138.play: When theirs are dry, for Romeo’s banishment.—

139.play: Take up those cords. The Nurse picks up the rope ladder. Poor ropes, you are beguiled,

140.play: Both you and I, for Romeo is exiled.

141.play: He made you for a highway to my bed,

142.play: But I, a maid, die maiden-widowèd.

143.play: Come, cords—come, nurse. I’ll to my wedding bed,

144.play: And death, not Romeo, take my maidenhead!

145.play: Hie to your chamber. I’ll find Romeo

146.play: To comfort you. I wot well where he is.

147.play: Hark you, your Romeo will be here at night.

148.play: I’ll to him. He is hid at Lawrence’ cell.

149.play: O, find him!    Giving the Nurse a ring. Give this ring to my true knight

150.play: And bid him come to take his last farewell. They exit.

2.play: Romeo, come forth; come forth, thou fearful man.

3.play: Affliction is enamored of thy parts,

4.play: And thou art wedded to calamity.

5.play: [Enter Romeo.]

6.play: Father, what news? What is the Prince’s doom?

7.play: What sorrow craves acquaintance at my hand

8.play: That I yet know not?

9.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Too familiar

10.play: Is my dear son with such sour company.

11.play: I bring thee tidings of the Prince’s doom.

12.play: What less than doomsday is the Prince’s doom?

13.play: A gentler judgment vanished from his lips:
notes: vanish'd] Ktly. I have never met with any sense of 'vanish' but its ordinary one, which certainly will not suit here. We should therefore, I think, read issued or some word of similar meaning. It is curious that Massinger seems to have taken ‘vanish’d’ on Sh.’s authority. 'Upon those lips from which those sweet words vanish’d.'—Reneg., v. 5. We have, however, in Lucrece: 'To make more vent for passage of her breath, Which, thronging through her lips, so vanisheth As smoke from Ætna, that in air consumes.' But the breath is material.

14.play: Not body’s death, but body’s banishment.

15.play: Ha, banishment? Be merciful, say “death,”

16.play: For exile hath more terror in his look,
notes: exile] Walker [‘Vers.,’ p. 291, cites this word in these passages as an example under] Art. lix. There are a number of dissyllabic verbs and adjectives,—the verbs more especially, I think, in the form of the past participle,—which, though at present they are accented on the latter syllable exclusively, have, in our old poets, an accent,—though of course an unequal one,—on both syllables; the principal one being shifted ad libitum from the one syllable to the other.

17.play: Much more than death. Do not say “banishment.”

18.play: Here from Verona art thou banishèd.

19.play: Be patient, for the world is broad and wide.

20.play: There is no world without Verona walls

21.play: But purgatory, torture, hell itself.

22.play: Hence “banishèd” is “banished from the world,”
notes: 'banished'] DEL. ('Lex.'). The repetition of the same word at the end of several successive lines is in Sh.'s style; and those edd. who adopt 'banishment' from (Q.) sacrifice to their own grammatical precision a perfectly Shaksperian inaccuracy of speech, originating in Romeo's passion. ULR. Romeo in his wild agony retains the word, which Lorenzo had just used, and which evokes the outpouring of his rage, with the obstinacy of passion, and uses the hated word even where the calm speech of every-day life would certainly say 'banishment.'

23.play: And world’s exile is death. Then “banishèd”

24.play: Is death mistermed. Calling death “banishèd,”

25.play: Thou cutt’st my head off with a golden ax

26.play: And smilest upon the stroke that murders me.

27.play: O deadly sin, O rude unthankfulness!

28.play: Thy fault our law calls death, but the kind prince,

29.play: Taking thy part, hath rushed aside the law
notes: rush'd] KTLV. Would not push'd be better? As in Hen. V: I, i, 5: But that the scambling and unquiet time Did push it out of further question.'

30.play: And turned that black word “death” to “banishment.”

31.play: This is dear mercy, and thou seest it not.
notes: dear mercy] STEEV. (Q.) reads 'mere mercy,' i.e., absolute mercy. [SING.

32.play: ’Tis torture and not mercy. Heaven is here
notes: heaven is here] STEEV. From this, and the foregoing speech of Romeo, Dryden has borrowed, in his beautiful paraphrase of Chaucer's Palamon and Arcite: 'Heaven is not, but where Emily abides And where she's absent, all is hell besides.' [SING. COLERIDGE ('Lit. Rem.' vol. ii, p. 157). All deep passions are a sort of atheists, that believe no future.

33.play: Where Juliet lives, and every cat and dog

34.play: And little mouse, every unworthy thing,

35.play: Live here in heaven and may look on her,

36.play: But Romeo may not. More validity,
notes: validity] STEEV. This is employed to signify worth or value in Lear I, i, 83. [SING.

37.play: More honorable state, more courtship lives
notes: courtship] SING. (ed. 1). By courtship, courtesy, courtly behavior is meant. Bullokar defines ‘compliment to be ceremony, court-ship, fine behavior.’ See also Cotgrave in Curiasnie and Curialite; and Florio in Cortegianita. ‘Would I might never excel a Dutch skipper in courtship, if I did not put distaste into my carriage of purpose.’—Sir Giles Goosecap. Again, in the same play: ‘My lord, my want of courtship makes me fear I should be rude.’

38.play: In carrion flies than Romeo. They may seize

39.play: On the white wonder of dear Juliet’s hand

40.play: And steal immortal blessing from her lips,

41.play: Who even in pure and vestal modesty
notes: Who .... ‘banished’?] CAMBR. Instead of the lines which he put in the margin, Pope inserted the following, copied with some alterations from (Qa): ‘But Romeo may not, he is banished! O father, hadst thou no strong poison mixt, No sharp ground knife, no present means of death, But banishment to torture me withal?’

42.play: Still blush, as thinking their own kisses sin;

43.play: But Romeo may not; he is banishèd.

44.play: Flies may do this, but I from this must fly.

45.play: They are free men, but I am banishèd.

46.play: And sayest thou yet that exile is not death?

47.play: Hadst thou no poison mixed, no sharp-ground knife,

48.play: No sudden mean of death, though ne’er so mean,

50.play: O friar, the damnèd use that word in hell.

51.play: Howling attends it. How hast thou the heart,

52.play: Being a divine, a ghostly confessor,

53.play: A sin absolver, and my friend professed,

54.play: To mangle me with that word “banishèd”?
notes: word] WHITE. The reading of (Q1) has been hitherto retained, although the change in Q2 seems plainly to have been made to avoid the unpleasant recurrence of 'word,' unemphasized, three times in four lines, twice at the end of lines spoken by the same character. [DYCE (ed. 2).

55.play: Thou fond mad man, hear me a little speak.
notes: fond] COLL. (ed. 1). 'Fond' is, of course, here, as in many other places, foolish. [Uuds.

56.play: O, thou wilt speak again of banishment.

57.play: I’ll give thee armor to keep off that word,

notes: Adversity's...banished] MAL. So in Romeus and Juliet, the Friar says:'Vertue is alwayes thrall to troubles and annoye,But wisedome in adversitie findes cause of quiet joye.'See also Lyly's Euphues, 1580: 'Thou sayest banishment is better to the freeborne. There be many meates which are sowre in the mouth and sweet in the maw; but if thou mingle them with sweet sawces, they yeeld both a pleasant taste and wholesome nourishment. I speake this to this end; that though thy exile seeme grievous to thee, yet, guiding thyselve with the rules of philosophy, it shall be more tolerable.' [Sing. Hal.

59.play: To comfort thee, though thou art banishèd.

61.play: Unless philosophy can make a Juliet,

62.play: Displant a town, reverse a prince’s doom,

63.play: It helps not, it prevails not. Talk no more.

64.play: O, then I see that madmen have no ears.

65.play: How should they when that wise men have no eyes?

66.play: Let me dispute with thee of thy estate.
notes: Let me dispute] STEEV. That is, let me talk over your affairs, or the present state you are in. [Sing.] The same phrase, with the same meaning, occurs in The Winter’s Tale: IV, iv, 411.

67.play: Thou canst not speak of that thou dost not feel.

68.play: Wert thou as young as I, Juliet thy love,

69.play: An hour but married, Tybalt murderèd,

70.play: Doting like me, and like me banishèd,

71.play: Then mightst thou speak, then mightst thou tear thy hair

72.play: And fall upon the ground as I do now, Romeo throws himself down.

73.play: Taking the measure of an unmade grave. Knock within.

74.play: Arise. One knocks. Good Romeo, hide thyself.

75.play: Not I, unless the breath of heartsick groans,

76.play: Mistlike, enfold me from the search of eyes. Knock.

77.play: Hark, how they knock!—Who’s there?—Romeo, arise.

78.play: Thou wilt be taken.—Stay awhile.—Stand up. Knock.

79.play: Run to my study.—By and by.—God’s will,

80.play: What simpleness is this?—I come, I come. Knock.

81.play: Who knocks so hard? Whence come you? What’s your will?

83.play: Let me come in, and you shall know my errand.

85.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE, admitting the Nurse Welcome, then.

87.play: O holy friar, O, tell me, holy friar,

88.play: Where’s my lady’s lord? Where’s Romeo?

89.play: There on the ground, with his own tears made drunk.

90.play: O, he is even in my mistress’ case,

91.play: Just in her case. O woeful sympathy!

92.play: Piteous predicament! Even so lies she,

93.play: Blubb’ring and weeping, weeping and blubb’ring.—

94.play: Stand up, stand up. Stand an you be a man.

95.play: For Juliet’s sake, for her sake, rise and stand.

96.play: Why should you fall into so deep an O?

98.play: Ah sir, ah sir, death’s the end of all.

100.play: Spakest thou of Juliet? How is it with her?

101.play: Doth not she think me an old murderer,

102.play: Now I have stained the childhood of our joy

103.play: With blood removed but little from her own?

104.play: Where is she? And how doth she? And what says

105.play: My concealed lady to our canceled love?
notes: conceal'd, &c.] HEATH. The epithet conceal'd is to be understood, not of the person, but of the condition of the lady. So that the sense is, My la'y, whose being so, together with our marriage which made her so, is concealed from the world. [Sing. Huds. Clarke. WALKER ('Vers.,' p. 291) cites this word as an example under Art. lix. See above, line 13 of this scene.

106.play: O, she says nothing, sir, but weeps and weeps,

107.play: And now falls on her bed, and then starts up,

108.play: And “Tybalt” calls, and then on Romeo cries,

111.play: Shot from the deadly level of a gun,

112.play: Did murder her, as that name’s cursèd hand

113.play: Murdered her kinsman.—O, tell me, friar, tell me,

114.play: In what vile part of this anatomy

115.play: Doth my name lodge? Tell me, that I may sack

116.play: The hateful mansion. He draws his dagger.

117.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Hold thy desperate hand!

118.play: Art thou a man? Thy form cries out thou art.
notes: Art thou a man? &c.] MAL. Sh. has here closely followed Romeus and Juliet: 'Art thou, quoth he, a man? thy shape saith, so thou art; Thy crying and thy weping eyes denote a woman's hart,

119.play: Thy tears are womanish; thy wild acts denote

120.play: The unreasonable fury of a beast.
notes: a beast] CLARKE. One of the numerous instances where Sh. uses ‘beast’ as the antithesis to ‘man.’ In As You Like It, IV, iii, 47, Rosalind, disguised as Ganymede, and reading the words: ‘Whiles the eye of man did woo me,’ adds, ‘Meaning me a beast;’ as though that were the necessary sequence in opposition to ‘man.’

121.play: Unseemly woman in a seeming man,

122.play: And ill-beseeming beast in seeming both!

123.play: Thou hast amazed me. By my holy order,

124.play: I thought thy disposition better tempered.

125.play: Hast thou slain Tybalt? Wilt thou slay thyself,

126.play: And slay thy lady that in thy life lives,
notes: And slay, &c.] DEL. This reading is far preferable to that of (Q.) on account of the accent that falls on the second thy, and because of the Shakspearian collocation of life and lives.

127.play: By doing damnèd hate upon thyself?

128.play: Why railest thou on thy birth, the heaven, and earth,
notes: thy b irth] MAL. Romeo has not here railed on his birth, &c., though in his interview with the Friar, as described in the poem, he is made to do so: ‘Fyst Nature did he blame, the author of his lyfe, In which his joyes had been so scant, and sorowes aye so ryfe; The time and place of byrth he fersly did reprove, He cryed out (with open mouth) against the starres above,— On Fortune eke he raylde.’ Sh. copied the remonstrance of the Friar without reviewing the former part of his scene. He has, in other places, fallen into a similar inaccuracy by sometimes following and sometimes deserting his original. [Sing. Sta. White.ULR. It is true Sh. appears to have followed here the source of his plot a little too closely, but the oversight is not so great as the English critics assume; it can be very readily supposed that before the scene opens Romeo had done what Laurence now reproaches him with.WHITE. The omission in (Q.) of seventeen lines in this speech is due, without a doubt, to the hasty and surreptitious manner in which that edition was published, and not to the addition of them upon the revision of the play

129.play: Since birth and heaven and earth all three do meet

130.play: In thee at once, which thou at once wouldst lose?

131.play: Fie, fie, thou shamest thy shape, thy love, thy wit,

132.play: Which, like a usurer, abound’st in all

133.play: And usest none in that true use indeed

134.play: Which should bedeck thy shape, thy love, thy wit.

135.play: Thy noble shape is but a form of wax,

136.play: Digressing from the valor of a man;
notes: Digressing] BOSWELL. So in Richard II: V, iii, 65. Also in Barnabe Riche's Farewell: ‘Knowing that you should otherwise have used me than you have, you should have digressed and swared from your kinde.’ [Sing. Hal. STEEV. So in the 24th book of Homer’s Odyssey, translated by Chapman:

137.play: Thy dear love sworn but hollow perjury,

138.play: Killing that love which thou hast vowed to cherish;

139.play: Thy wit, that ornament to shape and love,

140.play: Misshapen in the conduct of them both,

141.play: Like powder in a skilless soldier’s flask,
notes: powder] STEEV. The ancient English soldiers, using match-locks instead of locks with flints, were obliged to carry a lighted match hanging at their belts, very near to the wooden flask in which they kept their powder. The same allusion occurs in Humours Ordinary, an old collection of English epigrams: 'When she his flask and touch-box set on fire, And till this hour the burning is not out.' [Sing. Huds. Knut. Hal. ULR. That flint-locks were in use in Sh.’s middle age a passage in Hen. V: II, i, 55, shows. So that this reference here to a match-lock seems to me another proof that this tragedy belongs to the earlier pieces of Sh., and was written probably six or eight years before Henry V (1599).

142.play: Is set afire by thine own ignorance,

143.play: And thou dismembered with thine own defense.
notes: And thou] JOHNSON. And thou torn to pieces with thine own weapons. [Sing. Huds.

144.play: What, rouse thee, man! Thy Juliet is alive,
notes: thy Juliet, &c.] Ulr. Here again we must suppose that Romeo, before the opening of this scene, had expressed the fear that Juliet may have been made sick or even killed by horror and pain at his deed.

145.play: For whose dear sake thou wast but lately dead:

146.play: There art thou happy. Tybalt would kill thee,

147.play: But thou slewest Tybalt: there art thou happy.

148.play: The law that threatened death becomes thy friend

149.play: And turns it to exile: there art thou happy.

150.play: A pack of blessings light upon thy back;

151.play: Happiness courts thee in her best array;

152.play: But, like a misbehaved and sullen wench,

153.play: Thou pouts upon thy fortune and thy love.
notes: pout'st upon] Knt. Is to put up used as to put aside?

154.play: Take heed, take heed, for such die miserable.

155.play: Go, get thee to thy love, as was decreed.

156.play: Ascend her chamber. Hence and comfort her.

157.play: But look thou stay not till the watch be set,

158.play: For then thou canst not pass to Mantua,

159.play: Where thou shalt live till we can find a time

160.play: To blaze your marriage, reconcile your friends,

161.play: Beg pardon of the Prince, and call thee back

162.play: With twenty hundred thousand times more joy

163.play: Than thou went’st forth in lamentation.—

164.play: Go before, nurse. Commend me to thy lady,

165.play: And bid her hasten all the house to bed,

166.play: Which heavy sorrow makes them apt unto.

168.play: O Lord, I could have stayed here all the night

169.play: To hear good counsel. O, what learning is!—

170.play: My lord, I’ll tell my lady you will come.

171.play: Do so, and bid my sweet prepare to chide.

172.play: Here, sir, a ring she bid me give you, sir. Nurse gives Romeo a ring.
notes: Here, sir] Coll. (ed. 2). The insertion of ‘sir’ twice in the line may have been intended to indicate the state of feeling of the Nurse.

173.play: Hie you, make haste, for it grows very late. She exits.

174.play: How well my comfort is revived by this!

175.play: Go hence, good night—and here stands all your state:
notes: here stands all] Johns. The whole of your fortune depends on this. [Sing. Huds. Sta.

176.play: Either be gone before the watch be set

177.play: Or by the break of day disguised from hence.

178.play: Sojourn in Mantua. I’ll find out your man,

179.play: And he shall signify from time to time

180.play: Every good hap to you that chances here.

181.play: Give me thy hand. ’Tis late. Farewell. Good night.

182.play: But that a joy past joy calls out on me,

183.play: It were a grief so brief to part with thee.

1.play: [Enter old Capulet, his Wife, and Paris.]

2.play: Things have fallen out, sir, so unluckily

3.play: That we have had no time to move our daughter.

4.play: Look you, she loved her kinsman Tybalt dearly,

5.play: And so did I. Well, we were born to die.

6.play: ’Tis very late. She’ll not come down tonight.

7.play: I promise you, but for your company,

8.play: I would have been abed an hour ago.

9.play: These times of woe afford no times to woo.—

10.play: Madam, good night. Commend me to your daughter.

11.play: I will, and know her mind early tomorrow.
notes: mew'd] DYCE. 'Mew is the place, whether it be abroad or in the house, in which the Hawk is put during the time she casts or doth change her Feathers.'—R. Holme's Academy of Armory and Blazon (Terms of Art used in Falconry, &c.), B, ii, cxlvi, p. 241. ULR. What delight Lady Capulet takes in choice phrases!

12.play: Tonight she’s mewed up to her heaviness.
notes: desperate tender] JOHNS. This means only bold, adventurous, as if he had said in vulgar phrase: 'I will speak a bold word, and venture to promise you my daughter.' [Sing. Hud$.* Hal. STEEV. So in The Weakest goes to the Wall, 1600: 'Witness this desperate tender of mine honour.' [Sing. Hal. DEL. Capulet uses 'desperate' with affected modesty, as though it appeared even to himself excessively bold. STA. I will make a confident offer or promise of my daughter's love.

13.play: Sir Paris, I will make a desperate tender

14.play: Of my child’s love. I think she will be ruled

15.play: In all respects by me. Nay, more, I doubt it not.—

16.play: Wife, go you to her ere you go to bed.

17.play: Acquaint her here of my son Paris’ love,

18.play: And bid her—mark you me?—on Wednesday next—

19.play: But soft, what day is this?

20.play: PARIS: Monday, my lord.

21.play: Monday, ha ha! Well, Wednesday is too soon.

22.play: O’ Thursday let it be.—O’ Thursday, tell her,

23.play: She shall be married to this noble earl.—
notes: We'll keep] MOMMSEN. We should retain Well of Q, instead of We'll in (Q.), Q3; and in all our eds, Capulet, who had appointed the coming Thursday for the wedding, asks his wife, 'Will you be ready? do you like this haste?' Whereat the Lady makes a gesture of horror at the supposition that she can so soon be ready

24.play: Will you be ready? Do you like this haste?

25.play: We’ll keep no great ado: a friend or two.

26.play: For hark you, Tybalt being slain so late,

27.play: It may be thought we held him carelessly,

28.play: Being our kinsman, if we revel much.

29.play: Therefore we’ll have some half a dozen friends,

30.play: And there an end. But what say you to Thursday?

31.play: My lord, I would that Thursday were tomorrow.

32.play: Well, get you gone. O’ Thursday be it, then.

33.play: To Lady Capulet. Go you to Juliet ere you go to bed.

34.play: Prepare her, wife, against this wedding day.—
notes: 35. Afore . . . night] DYCE (ed. 1). The arrangement of Theobald’s [followed by Dyce himself in (ed. 2)] is evidently against the author’s intention; and compare the close of the preceding scene. Enter Romeo] MAL. They appeared, probably, in the balcony, erected on the old English stage. [Sing. Hud.

35.play: Farewell, my lord.—Light to my chamber, ho!—

36.play: Afore me, it is so very late that we

37.play: May call it early by and by.—Good night. They exit.

2.play: Wilt thou be gone? It is not yet near day.

3.play: It was the nightingale, and not the lark,

4.play: That pierced the fearful hollow of thine ear.

5.play: Nightly she sings on yond pomegranate tree.
notes: Nightly] STEEV. This is not merely a poetical supposition. It is said of the nightingale that, if undisturbed, she sits and sings upon the same tree for many weeks together. [SINGER adds: As almost all birds sing only during the period of incubation, this may be accounted for; the male bird sings near where the female is sitting.] What Eustathius, however, has observed relative to a fig-tree mentioned by Homer in his 12th Odyssey, may be applied to the passage before us: ‘These particularities, which seem of no consequence, have a very good effect in poetry, as they give the relation an air of truth and probability. For what can induce a poet to mention such a tree if the tree were not there in reality?’ [Sta. KNT. In the description of the garden in Chaucer's translation of the ‘Romaunt of the Rose,’ the pomegranate is first mentioned amongst the fruit-trees. The ‘orchard of pomegranates with pleasant fruits’ was one of the beautiful objects described by Solomon in his Canticles. Amongst the fruit-bearing trees, the pomegranate is in some respects the most beautiful; and therefore, in the south of Europe

6.play: Believe me, love, it was the nightingale.

7.play: It was the lark, the herald of the morn,
notes: the lark] KNT. Sh.’s power in describing natural objects is unequalled in this beautiful scene, which, as we think, was amongst his very early productions. The Venus and Adonis, published in 1593, is also full of this power. Compare the following passage with the description of morning in the scene before us: ‘Lo! here the gentle lark, weary of rest, From his moist cabinet mounts up on high, And wakes the morning, from whose silver breast The sun ariseth in his majesty; Who doth the world so gloriously behold That cedar-tops and hills seem burnish’d gold.’ [Huds.

8.play: No nightingale. Look, love, what envious streaks

9.play: Do lace the severing clouds in yonder east.

10.play: Night’s candles are burnt out, and jocund day
notes: Night’s candles are] BLAKEWAY. Thus Sophocles: ἄκριας νυκτὸς, ὑπνῆ ἔστραπον δαμπτειρες οὐκέτ ἤθον.—Ajax, 285. [Sing. STA. [thus translates]: ‘At dead of night, What time the evening tapers had expired.’ But Sh. certainly meant the stars, while Sophocles seems only to have thought of the less poetical lamps of earth. JEFFREY (Edin. Rev., Aug. 1817). If the advocates for the grand style object to this expression, we shall not stop to defend it; but to us it seems equally beautiful, as it is obvious and natural, to a person coming out of a lighted chamber into the pale dawn. The word candle, we admit, is rather homely in modern language, while lamp is sufficiently dignified for poetry. The moon hangs her silver lamp on high in every school-boy’s copy of verses; and she could not be called the candle of heaven without manifest absurdity. Such are the caprices of usage. Yet we like the passage before us much better as it is than if the candles were changed into lamps. If we should read, ‘The lamps of heaven are quenched,’ or ‘wax dim,’ the whole charm of the expression would be lost—our fancies would no longer be re-

11.play: Stands tiptoe on the misty mountain-tops.

12.play: I must be gone and live, or stay and die.

13.play: Yond light is not daylight, I know it, I.

14.play: It is some meteor that the sun exhaled

15.play: To be to thee this night a torchbearer
notes: torch-bearer] TODD. Compare Sidney’s Arcadia (ed. 13) p. 109 : ‘The moon, then full (not thinking scorn to be a torch-bearer to such beauty), guided her steps.’ And Sir J. Davies’s Orchestra, 1596, st. vii, of the sun: ‘When the great torch-bearer of heaven was gone Downe in a maske unto the Ocean’s court.’ And Drayton’s Eng. Heroic. Epist., p. 221, where the moon is described with the stars: ‘Attending on her as torch-bearers.’ [Sing.

16.play: And light thee on thy way to Mantua.

17.play: Therefore stay yet. Thou need’st not to be gone.

18.play: Let me be ta’en; let me be put to death.
notes: Let me be ta'en] SING. quotes BOSWELL that this speech is better in Q1.

19.play: I am content, so thou wilt have it so.

20.play: I’ll say yon gray is not the morning’s eye;

21.play: ’Tis but the pale reflex of Cynthia’s brow.
notes: Cynthia’s brow] COLL. [‘Notes and Emend.’]. Cynthia’s ‘brow’ would not occasion a ‘pale reflex,’ and by the omission of one letter the light is at once cleared,—‘Cynthia’s bow.’ SING. (‘Sh. Vindicated’). The (MS.) correction is quite unexceptionable, as an easy amendment of an evident misprint. ULR. Collier’s (MS.) correction recommends itself for this reason, that the reflex of Cynthia’s ‘bow’ properly refers only to the setting moon (Diana, who turns her back upon the lovers), whereas the reflex of Cynthia’s ‘brow’ or ‘eye’ would indicate that the moon was just rising. SING. (ed. 2). The r is deleted in my F4. STA. The (MS.) substitution of bow is a very happy conjecture, and one which certainly affords a better reading than the old text. It must be remembered, however, that brow is the word in all the ancient copies, and that Sh. has allowed himself great latitude in the use of it in other places. In Othello we meet with the ‘brow of the sea,’ and in King John with the ‘brow of night.’ [Dyce (ed. 2). DYCE (ed. 1). ‘Brow’ suits the context (‘eye’) better than ‘bow.’ COLL. (ed. 2). Such a confirmation [the erasure of the r in Singer’s F4], sup-

22.play: Nor that is not the lark whose notes do beat

23.play: The vaulty heaven so high above our heads.

24.play: I have more care to stay than will to go.

25.play: Come death and welcome. Juliet wills it so.

26.play: How is ’t, my soul? Let’s talk. It is not day.

27.play: It is, it is. Hie hence, begone, away!

28.play: It is the lark that sings so out of tune,

29.play: Straining harsh discords and unpleasing sharps.

30.play: Some say the lark makes sweet division.
notes: division] NARES. To make divisions in music is to run a simple strain into a great variety of shorter notes to the same modulation. [Dyce. SING. A division, in music, is a variation of melody upon some given fundamental harmony. See 1 Hen. IV: III, i, 210: ‘Sung by a fair queen in a summer’s bower, With ravishing division to her lute.’ This verse, Stephen Weston observes, might serve for a translation of a line in Horace: 'grataque feminis Imbelli cithara carmina divides.’ [Huds. KNT. A number of quick notes sung to one syllable; a kind of warbling, which prevailed in vocal music till rather recently. [Verp.] Handel, governed by custom rather than by his own better taste, introduces divisions into many of his airs and choruses. [Hal.] Steevens, in his note on this word, mistakes the meaning entirely. STA. It is what we now term variation; where, instead of one note, two, three or more notes are sung to one syllable or to one chord. [White, subs.

31.play: This doth not so, for she divideth us.

32.play: Some say the lark and loathèd toad changed eyes.
notes: loathed toad] HEATH. If the toad and lark had changed voices, the unnatural croak of the latter would have been no sign of the appearance of day, and consequently no signal for her lover’s departure. [Sing. Corn. Verp. Huds. Cham. Ste. WARB. The toad, having very fine eyes, and the lark very ugly ones, was the

33.play: O, now I would they had changed voices too,

34.play: Since arm from arm that voice doth us affray,

35.play: Hunting thee hence with hunt’s-up to the day.
notes: hunts-up] STEEV. The tune anciently played to wake and collect the hunters. In Drayton's Polyolbion, Song 13th: ‘But hunts-up to the morn the feather’d sylvans sing.’ [Sing. Huds. White, Clarke.] RITSON. Puttenham, in his Art of English Poesy, 1589, speaking of one Gray, says: ‘What good estimation did he grow into with King Henry [the Eighth] . . . for making certaine merry ballads, whereof one chiefly was “The Hunte is up, the Hunte is up”.’ [Sing. Knt. Huds.] MAL. It also signified a morning song to a new-married woman, the day after her marriage, and is certainly used here in that sense. See Cotgrave’s Dict., s. v. Resveil. [Sing. Huds. Clarke.] DOUCE. It is not improbable that the following was the identical song composed by the person of the name of Gray mentioned in Ritson’s note. It occurs in a collection entitled Hunting, hawking, &c. There was likewise a country-dance with a similar title. CHO. {The hunt is up, the hunt is up, Sing merrily wee, the hunt is up; The birds they sing, The Deare they fing, Hey, nony, nony-no; The hounds they crye, The hunters fye, Hey trollio, trollolio. The hunt is up, w' supra.

36.play: O, now begone. More light and light it grows.

37.play: More light and light, more dark and dark our woes.

38.play: [Enter Nurse.]
notes: Enter Nurse.] COLL. This part of the play, in (Q₁), reads exactly as if it had been hastily made up from imperfect notes, and not printed from any authentic copy. Our text, here as elsewhere, is generally that of Q₂.

39.play: NURSE: Madam.

40.play: JULIET: Nurse?

41.play: Your lady mother is coming to your chamber.

42.play: The day is broke; be wary; look about.      She exits.

43.play: Then, window, let day in, and let life out.

44.play: Farewell, farewell. One kiss and I’ll descend. They kiss, and Romeo descends.

45.play: Art thou gone so? Love, lord, ay husband, friend!
notes: my lord, my love, my friend] DYCE (ed. 1). I have preferred the reading of (Q1) because I have great doubts if the ‘ay’ is to be understood as equivalent to ‘yes’ (the usual old spelling of it in that sense being ‘I’). The editor of F1 altered it to ‘ah;’ for which perhaps it was intended. WHITE. Perhaps ‘ay’ is a misprint for ‘my.’ The reading of (Q1) has the advantage of ridding the line of the awkward and unpoetic word ‘husband,’ which is in no sense, except legally, a counterpart to ‘wife.’ But in the word ‘friend’ there was not that anticlimax in Sh.’s time that there is now. ‘Friend’ was then used to express the dearest possible relation, even between the sexes. It frequently occurs in that sense in the poem Romeus and Juliet; and in the very passage which is here dramatized, Juliet, in her distress that Romeo will neither remain with her, nor let her go with him, exclaims (and Sh. seems to have remembered it): ‘For whom am I become unto myself a foe, Disdayneth me, his steadfast frend, and skornes my frendship so. Nay, Romeus, nay, &c. ‘Then Romeus in armes his lady gan to folde, With frendly kisse, and ruthfully she gan her knight beholde. With solemne othe they both their sorrowful leave do take; They sweare no stormy troubles shall their steadye frendship shake.’

46.play: I must hear from thee every day in the hour,

47.play: For in a minute there are many days.

48.play: O, by this count I shall be much in years
notes: by this count] STEEV. ‘Certè ego, quæ fueram, te, discedente, puella, Protintus ut redeas, facta videbor anus.’—Ovid, Epist. [Her.], i, . [Sing.

49.play: Ere I again behold my Romeo.

notes: Farewell] S. WALKER (‘Vers.,’ p. 268). An exclamation, a form of address, or other word, or short phrase, detached in point of construction from the sentence

52.play: That may convey my greetings, love, to thee.

53.play: O, think’st thou we shall ever meet again?

54.play: I doubt it not; and all these woes shall serve

55.play: For sweet discourses in our times to come.

56.play: O God, I have an ill-divining soul!
notes: ill-divining soul] STEEV. This miserable prescience of futurity I have always regarded as a circumstance particularly beautiful. The same kind of warning from the mind, Romeo seems to have been conscious of, on his going to the entertainment at the house of Capulet. [Sing. Huds. Sta.] MAL. So in Venus and Adonis: 'The thought of it doth make my faint heart bleed; And fear doth teach it divination; I prophesy thy death.' [Knt.] KNT. Coleridge has some remarks upon Richard II : II, ii, 10, which we may properly quote here: 'Mark in this scene Sh.'s gentleness in touching the tender superstitions, the terra incognita of presentiments, in the human mind; and how sharp a line of distinction he commonly draws between these obscure forecastings of general experience in each individual, and the vulgar errors of mere tradition. Indeed, it may be taken once for all as the truth that Sh., in the absolute universality of his genius, always reverences whatever arises out of our moral nature. He never profanes his muse with a contemptuous reasoning away of the genuine and general, however unaccountable, feelings of mankind.'—‘Lit. Rem.,' vol. ii, p. 174. Sh. has exhibited the feeling under three different aspects in this play. When Romeo utters his presentiment before going to the masquerade, he is under the influence of his habitual melancholy,—the sentiment of unrequited love, which colours all his imagination with a gloomy foreshadowing of coming events. In the passage before us, when Juliet sees her husband 'As one dead in the bottom of a tomb,' we have 'the fear' which doth 'teach' her heart 'divination.' But Romeo in Act V has a presentiment directly contrary to the approaching catastrophe; and this arises out of his 'unaccustomed' animal spirits. All these states of mind are common to the imagination deeply stirred by passionate emotions. Nothing in all Sh.'s philosophy appears to us finer than the deceiving nature of Romeo's presages in the last Act as compared with the true-divining fears of Juliet.

57.play: Methinks I see thee, now thou art so low,

58.play: As one dead in the bottom of a tomb.

59.play: Either my eyesight fails or thou lookest pale.

60.play: And trust me, love, in my eye so do you.

61.play: Dry sorrow drinks our blood. Adieu, adieu.  He exits.
notes: Dry sorrow] CLARKE. The belief that grieving exhausts the blood and impairs the health is more than once alluded to by Sh. See Mid. N.'s D., III, ii, 97.

62.play: O Fortune, Fortune, all men call thee fickle.

63.play: If thou art fickle, what dost thou with him

64.play: That is renowned for faith? Be fickle, Fortune,

65.play: For then I hope thou wilt not keep him long,

notes: Enter Lady Capulet] MRS. JAMESON. In the dialogue between Juliet and her parents, and in the scenes with the Nurse, we seem to have before us the whole of her previous education and habits; we see her, on the one hand, kept in severe subjection by her austere parents, and, on the other, fondled and spoiled by a foolish old nurse—a situation perfectly accordant with the manners of the time. Then Lady Capulet comes sweeping by, with her train of velvet, her black hood, her fan and rosary—the very beau-ideal of a proud Italian matron of the fifteenth century, whose offer to poison Romeo, in revenge for the death of Tybalt, stamps her with one very characteristic trait of the age and the country. Yet she loves her daughter, and there is a touch of remorseful tenderness in her lamentations over her which adds to our impression of the timid softness of Juliet and the harsh subjection in which she has been kept. [Verp.

68.play: LADY CAPULET: Ho, daughter, are you up?

69.play: Who is ’t that calls? It is my lady mother.

70.play: Is she not down so late or up so early?

71.play: What unaccustomed cause procures her hither? Juliet descends.
notes: procures] WARB. Procures for brings. [Sing.

73.play: JULIET: Madam, I am not well.

74.play: Evermore weeping for your cousin’s death?

75.play: What, wilt thou wash him from his grave with tears?

76.play: An if thou couldst, thou couldst not make him live.

77.play: Therefore have done. Some grief shows much of love,

78.play: But much of grief shows still some want of wit.
notes: want of wit] ULR. It is thoroughly in keeping with Lady Capulet's heart.

79.play: Yet let me weep for such a feeling loss.

80.play: So shall you feel the loss, but not the friend

notes: Feeling] MOMMSEN. Suppose Sh. has for once committed a metrical error (bonus dormitat Homerus), what harm is there if a critic correct the same? Which shows a higher estimate of Sh., and of the nature of poetic forms in general, the critic who corrects here and there an error which Sh. himself perhaps overlooked, or he who attributes to the poet many hundreds of halting verses? I think the latter shows more reverence for the Printing Offices of the 16th and 17th centuries than for the art of the great poet. This respect for the printers is as false as it is convenient.

83.play: I cannot choose but ever weep the friend.

84.play: Well, girl, thou weep’st not so much for his death

85.play: As that the villain lives which slaughtered him.

87.play: LADY CAPULET: That same villain, Romeo.

89.play: Villain and he be many miles asunder.—
notes: asunder] KNTLY. I have placed a (!) at the end of this line; for Juliet is evidently speaking here in the ambiguous manner of her subsequent speeches. She means an indicative, but wishes her mother to understand her in the optative mood. The editors of the last century, not understanding this, have, without any authority, changed 'be' to are. I should be inclined to make an Aside of 'I do with all my heart,' as she pretends to plan his death.
he be] MOMMSEN. Be in consonance with he is very frequent in Sh. instead of are.

90.play: God pardon him. I do with all my heart,
notes: God . . . . girl] CAMBR. Instead of this passage, Pope, printing, as he says, 'more agreeably to the first edition,' gave as follows: 'La. Cap. Content thee girl. If I could find a man, I soon would send to Mantua where he is, And give him such an unaccustom'd dram That he should soon keep Tybalt company. Jul. Find you the means, and I'll find such a man, For while he lives, my heart shall ne'er be light 'Till I behold him--dead--is my poor heart, Thus for a kinsman vext? La. Cap. Well, let that pass. I come to bring thee joyful tidings, girl.'

91.play: And yet no man like he doth grieve my heart.

92.play: That is because the traitor murderer lives.

93.play: Ay, madam, from the reach of these my hands.

94.play: Would none but I might venge my cousin’s death!

95.play: We will have vengeance for it, fear thou not.

96.play: Then weep no more. I’ll send to one in Mantua,

97.play: Where that same banished runagate doth live,

98.play: Shall give him such an unaccustomed dram

99.play: That he shall soon keep Tybalt company.

100.play: And then, I hope, thou wilt be satisfied.

101.play: Indeed, I never shall be satisfied

102.play: With Romeo till I behold him—dead—

103.play: Is my poor heart, so for a kinsman vexed.
notes: Is my poor heart] Ktly. By connecting this phrase with the preceding 'dead,' it is manifest they [both Qq and folio—followed by all the edd.] did not understand the ambiguous language of Juliet.

104.play: Madam, if you could find out but a man

105.play: To bear a poison, I would temper it,

106.play: That Romeo should, upon receipt thereof,

107.play: Soon sleep in quiet. O, how my heart abhors

108.play: To hear him named and cannot come to him

109.play: To wreak the love I bore my cousin
notes: my cousin] Mal. The word omitted is probably an epithet to cousin; such as,—my murdered cousin. [Dyce, White.

110.play: Upon his body that hath slaughtered him.

111.play: Find thou the means, and I’ll find such a man.

112.play: But now I’ll tell thee joyful tidings, girl.

113.play: And joy comes well in such a needy time.
notes: a needy time] Walker ['Crit.,' vol. ii, p. 8o. This line is cited as one of the instances where awful, dreadful, needful, and the like, are used in an active sense. He therefore cites (Q.)]. Middleton, W. of Solomon Faraphrased, Dyce, vol. v, p. 346: 'Decaying things be needful of repair.' I have met with needful, in this sense, in Walter Scott. Perhaps he caught it from Sh.; or is it a Scotticism? Debates in the 'Free Presbyterian Assembly,' as reported in the Glasgow Constitutional of May 24, 1843; Dr. Buchanan says: ' every unprejudiced mind would admit that, if a Church stood in need of advice, the Church of England at this time was eminently needful of it.' Lettsom [in a foot-note to the foregoing] . . . does not needy rather mean beggarly, poverty-stricken? [Dyce (ed. 2).

114.play: What are they, beseech your Ladyship?

115.play: Well, well, thou hast a careful father, child,

116.play: One who, to put thee from thy heaviness,

117.play: Hath sorted out a sudden day of joy

118.play: That thou expects not, nor I looked not for.

119.play: Madam, in happy time! What day is that?
notes: in happy time] JOHNS. A la bonne heure. This phrase was interjected when the hearer was not quite so well pleased as the speaker. [Sing. HudS. SING. Bishop Lowth uses it in his Letter to Warburton, p. 101: 'And may I not hope then for the honour of your lordship's animadversions? In good time: when the candid examiner understands Latin a little better, and when your lordship has a competent knowledge of Hebrew.' [Clarke.

120.play: Marry, my child, early next Thursday morn

121.play: The gallant, young, and noble gentleman,

122.play: The County Paris, at Saint Peter’s Church
notes: The County Paris] FARMER. It is remarked that ‘Paris, though in one place called Earl, is most commonly styled the Countie in this play. Sh. seems to have preferred, for some reason or other, the Italian Comte to our Count; perhaps he took it from the old English novel, from which he is said to have taken his plot.' He certainly did so; Paris is there first styled a young Earle, and afterwards Counte, Countee, County, according to the unsettled orthography of the time. [Hcl. SING. County or countie was the usual term for an earl in Sh.'s time. [Sta.] Paris is in this play first styled a young earle. [HudS.] So Baret, 'a countie, or an earle, comes, un comte,' and 'a countie or earldome, comitatus.'

123.play: Shall happily make thee there a joyful bride.

124.play: Now, by Saint Peter’s Church, and Peter too,

125.play: He shall not make me there a joyful bride!

126.play: I wonder at this haste, that I must wed

127.play: Ere he that should be husband comes to woo.

128.play: I pray you, tell my lord and father, madam,

129.play: I will not marry yet, and when I do I swear
notes: I swear] COLL. [‘Notes and Emend’.] This is erased, perhaps, as not adding to the force of Juliet's expression, hardly consistent with the delicacy of her character, and certainly destructive of the measure. ULR. But her mother's announcement has manifestly excited Juliet to the highest pitch, as is shown by the imprudent abruptness and obstinacy with which she opposes the wishes of her parents, and which could only make bad worse. She constantly displays a temper as passionate and as easily enkindled as Romeo's. In such a state of excitement words could easily escape her which she would not use

130.play: It shall be Romeo, whom you know I hate,

131.play: Rather than Paris. These are news indeed!
notes: These are news indeed] COLL. (ed. 2). These words indicate the surprise of Lady Capulet at the intelligence she has just heard, and they join on with the utmost exactness to what follows of her speech. Strange to say, the blunder of giving this exclamation to Juliet ' has never, in modern times, been detected, but the matter is set right in the (MS.). The mistake, when pointed out, seems to correct itself.HUDS. This change by Collier's (MS.), though not necessary to the sense, seems well worthy of being considered.DYCE (‘Strictures,’ &c., 1859). It seems almost impossible that any one should read the passage, as it stands in the old copies, without perceiving that Juliet's exclamation has reference to what her mother has said a little before, ‘But now I'll tell the joyful tidings, girl.’

132.play: Here comes your father. Tell him so yourself,

133.play: And see how he will take it at your hands.

135.play: When the sun sets, the earth doth drizzle dew,
notes: the air doth drizzle dew] MAL. The reading of Q₂Q₃Ff is philosophically true, and perhaps ought to be preferred. I suspected, when this note was written, that earth was the poet's word, and a line in the Rape of Lucrece strongly supports that reading: ‘But as the earth doth weep the sun being set.’ [Sing. Huds. Sta. Hal.STEEV. When our author in A Mid. Sum. N. D. says: ‘And when she [the moon] weeps, weeps every little flower,’ he only means that every little flower is moistened with dew, as if with tears, and not that the flower itself drizzles dew. This passage sufficiently explains how the earth, in the quotation from the Rape of Lucrece, may be said to weep. [Sing. Hal.RITSON. That Sh. thought it was the air and not the earth that drizzled dew, is evident from other passages. So in King John: ‘Before the dew of evening fall.’ [Sing.COLL. (ed. 1). Malone fully justifies ‘earth’ (though he prints air) by the line from Sh.'s Lucrece.HUDS. This is scientifically true; poetically, it would seem better to read air instead of earth.DYCE. As to the passage from our author's Lucrece, Steevens showed long ago that it did not ‘justify’ (what, indeed, could?) such an utter absurdity as ‘the EARTH DRIZZLING dew.’

136.play: But for the sunset of my brother’s son

138.play: How now, a conduit, girl? What, still in tears?

139.play: Evermore show’ring? In one little body

140.play: Thou counterfeits a bark, a sea, a wind.

141.play: For still thy eyes, which I may call the sea,

142.play: Do ebb and flow with tears; the bark thy body is,

143.play: Sailing in this salt flood; the winds thy sighs,

144.play: Who, raging with thy tears and they with them,

145.play: Without a sudden calm, will overset

147.play: Have you delivered to her our decree?

148.play: Ay, sir, but she will none, she gives you thanks.

149.play: I would the fool were married to her grave.

150.play: Soft, take me with you, take me with you, wife.

151.play: How, will she none? Doth she not give us thanks?
notes: how! . . . none?] MOMMSEN. This is one sentence, and equivalent to 'What do you mean by that, that she will none?' which is much more characteristic of the violent Capulet than the tame and disjointed 'How? Will she none?'

152.play: Is she not proud? Doth she not count her blessed,

153.play: Unworthy as she is, that we have wrought

154.play: So worthy a gentleman to be her bride?
notes: her bridegroom] MOMMSEN. Q2 has here the noteworthy reading Bride. I must leave it undecided whether or not this is also to be found elsewhere, but will call attention to the fact that bride is also in our language dialectic for bridegroom, although Grimm (Dt. Wörterb, ii, p. 332) considers it as a transferring of the idea. It was also Middle High German. Compare Müller Mhd. Wörterb., p. 273 f, where a passage is cited from Gotfrt in reference to Christ as the spiritual Bridegroom: 'Vil maniges reinen herczen trût, Vil maniger reinen megde brût.' There is, in addition, the metrical reason that, in this play, supernumerary syllables [aer klingende Ausgang] are comparatively rare, and almost wholly confined to light final syllables. I therefore consider it better to disregard the sophistications of the compositor of Q3.

155.play: Not proud you have, but thankful that you have.

156.play: Proud can I never be of what I hate,

157.play: But thankful even for hate that is meant love.
notes: is meant love] KNT. That is, meant as love.

158.play: How, how, how, how? Chopped logic? What is this?
notes: chop-logic] STEEV. This term, hitherto divided into two words, I have given as one, it being, as I learn from The XXIIII Orders of Knaves, b1. 1, no date, a nickname: 'Choplogyk is he that whan his mayster rebuketh his servaunt for his deſautes, he will gyve hym xx wordes for one, or elles he wyll bydde the devylles pater noster in scylence.' [Sing. Huds. Sta. Clarke.] In The Contentyon betwyxte Churchyeard and Camell, &c., 1560, this word also occurs: 'But you wyl choplegick And be Bee-to-busse,' &c. [Hal.

159.play: “Proud,” and “I thank you,” and “I thank you not,”

160.play: And yet “not proud”? Mistress minion you,

161.play: Thank me no thankings, nor proud me no prouds,

162.play: But fettle your fine joints ’gainst Thursday next
notes: fettle] NARES. To go intently upon any business. Certainly an English word, being acknowledged by our old dictionary-makers. Phillips has ‘to fettle to, to go about, or enter upon a business.’ Kersey, as usual, copies him. Coles has, ‘to fettle, ae accingere ad aliquid, aggredior.’ Of uncertain derivation, though it seems like a corruption of settle. It was, probably, always a familiar, undignified word, and still exists as a provincial term. Ray speaks of it as in common use in the north, and defines it, ‘to set or go all out at aything, to dress or prepare.’ The only old author hitherto quoted for it is Hall, Satires, b, iv, sat. 6: ‘But scils his team, and fettleth to the warre’ [cited by Staunton, Keightley]. I can add Sylvester: ‘They to their long hard journey fettling them, Leaving Samaria and Jerusalem.’—Maiden's Blush [cited by Keightley]. Swift also used it in his Directions to Servants. See Todd. In the Glossary to Tim Bobbin, we have fettle, explained as a substantive by ‘dress, case, condition.’ STA. To fettle means to prepare, to make ready: ‘When the sheriff saw Little John bend his bow, He fettled him to be gone.’—PERCY's Reliques, i, 92, ed. 1767. The word does not occur again in our author, and, curiously enough, it has been overlooked in this passage by every editor from Rowe downwards. WHITE. The misprint is so very easy, and both words are so well adapted to the passage, that there may be some doubt as to what Sh. wrote. But the weight of authority is in favor of ‘fettle.’ KNTLY. I cannot conceive why the editors all read settle for ‘fettle.’

163.play: To go with Paris to Saint Peter’s Church,

164.play: Or I will drag thee on a hurdle thither.

165.play: Out, you green-sickness carrion! Out, you baggage!

notes: tallow-face] STEEV. Such was the indelicacy of the age of Sh. that authors were not contented only to employ these terms of abuse in their own original performances, but even felt no reluctance to introduce them in their versions of the most chaste and elegant of the Greek or Roman poets. Stanyhurst, the translator of Virgil, 1582, makes Dido call Æneas hedge-brat, cullion and tar-breech in the course of one speech. [Huds.] Nay, in the Interlude of The Repentance of Mary Magdalene, 1567, Mary Magdalene says to one of her attendants: ‘Horeson, I beshrowe your heart, are you here?’ [Sing. WHITE. It is intended, of course, that Capulet should be vituperative; but the terms which he uses did not excite the disgust in Sh.'s time that they do now. ‘Carcass’ ard ‘carrion,’ and even kindred words that we do not now write or speak,

167.play: LADY CAPULET: Fie, fie, what, are you mad?

169.play: Good father, I beseech you on my knees,

170.play: Hear me with patience but to speak a word.

171.play: Hang thee, young baggage, disobedient wretch!

172.play: I tell thee what: get thee to church o’ Thursday,

173.play: Or never after look me in the face.

174.play: Speak not; reply not; do not answer me.

175.play: My fingers itch.—Wife, we scarce thought us blessed

176.play: That God had lent us but this only child,
notes: lent us] White. ['lent'] is manifestly a misprint due to the mistaking of a long s ('f') for '1.' Dyce (ed. 2). Though I here follow the earliest authority, I see nothing objectionable in the reading of the later old eds. Clarke. We think it possible that 'left' may have been originally written by the author here, because in a previous scene Capulet speaks as if he had had other children born to him, who died young (I, ii, 14).

177.play: But now I see this one is one too much,

178.play: And that we have a curse in having her.
notes: a curse] White. [Q₁] has 'crosse,' &c., for which the later reading is possibly a misprint.

hilding] Nares. A base, low, menial wretch; derived by some from hind—derling, a Devonshire word signifying degenerate; by others, from the Saxon (see Todd's Johnson). Perhaps, after all, no more originally than a corruption of hireling or hindling, diminutive of hind. It was applied to women as well as men. Cham. Sax. hyldan, to crouch.

180.play: NURSE: God in heaven bless her!

181.play: You are to blame, my lord, to rate her so.

182.play: And why, my Lady Wisdom? Hold your tongue.

183.play: Good Prudence, smatter with your gossips, go.
notes: Good prudence] DEL. Just as ‘prudence’ is here personified as a female, it was in The Temp. II, i, 286, personified as a male.

188.play: Utter your gravity o’er a gossip’s bowl,

189.play: For here we need it not.

190.play: LADY CAPULET: You are too hot.

191.play: CAPULET: God’s bread, it makes me mad.
notes: God’s bread . . . company] ULR. Malone manufactured a text out of the various readings of the old eds., apparently only because the text of Q,Q, and Ff appeared too incorrect in its versification. But this incorrectness admirably suits old Capulet’s blustering outburst of rage, and the imperfection thereby becomes an excellence.WHITE. Perhaps the composite reading given by Malone very nearly approaches what Sh. wrote on the revision of the play.KITELY. I arrange this passage in accordance with the old eds., except (Q), the reading of which is different, and is not verse at all. I omit ‘time’ as injurious to the symmetry of the language; for the words in the first two lines run, as will be seen, pairwise. It may have been a marginal note explanatory of ‘tide.’ As to line 177, being of six feet, three such have already occurred in this scene.CLARKE. Here the solemn expression put into the mouth of the furious Capulet is in strict accordance with what we still hear in Italy from the mouths of angry quarrelers; who often use its equivalent in the words, ‘Per l’ Ostia !’

192.play: Day, night, hour, tide, time, work, play,

193.play: Alone, in company, still my care hath been

194.play: To have her matched. And having now provided
notes: having now provided] MAL. There is a passage in Wily Beguil’d so nearly resembling this that one poet must have copied from the other. Wily Beguil’d was on the stage before 1596, being mentioned by Nashe in his Have With You to Saffron Walden, printed in that year. [Sing. COLL. (ed. 2). There is no doubt that the author of Wily Beguiled did imitate Sh.; but although Wily Beguiled was in existence before 1596, we have no copy of it earlier than 1606. Malone, as usual, committed various errors in his citation, and among others printed ‘puling’ pouting, which so far lessens the resemblance. We can the more readily believe that the author of Wily Beguiled was the imitator in this case, because ‘another part of the same comedy is directly borrowed from ‘The Merch. of Ven.,’ V, i.

196.play: Of fair demesnes, youthful, and nobly ligned,
notes: train’d] ULR. I prefer ‘allied,’ because it follows almost of necessity, from the character of old Capulet, that, in the enumeration of Paris’s advantages, he would not forget his kinship to the Prince.

nobly trained] MOMMSEN. This [liand of Q.] might be metrically tolerated, but it might be that Capulet, having described Paris as a gentleman of noble parentage, should go on to enumerate several other of his qualities, and then once more speak of him as nobly allied, which would be simply iterating what he had just said, as e. g. in Marlowe, ii, p. 212: ‘His name is Spenser; he is well allied.’ Rhetorical pleonasms—like ‘The spring, the head, the fountain of your blood’—Macb., II, iii, 103; ‘Being with his presence glutted, gorg’d and full’—I Hen. IV: III, ii, 84; ‘Uncapable of pity, void and empty From any dram of pity’—Mer. of Ven., IV, i, 5—would prove nothing in this passage, where various different features are introduced. Therefore most of the later edd. have adopted nobly train’d. But by liand might have been meant lined, an orthography which, it is true, I have not met with elsewhere, but which is at times found in the case of ire, thus uitierly for entirely, wiars for wires (in How to Choose a Good Wife from a Bad, 1608, 4to); fier, hier, squier not seldom for fire, hire, squire; just as the reverse is often found, lide, tride for lied, tried, &c. Then too nobly lined might refer to his purse; although loin’d would suit youthful better, and the rude style of the speaker. The spelling ryall for royal is to be found in Heywood. Perchance, can gryans, lians be found, as a kind of drawling, for groins, loins, like quire for choir? Until it can be proved by examples which of the two words is meant, we must fall back upon the conclusion that allied is assuredly corrupt.

197.play: Stuffed, as they say, with honorable parts,

198.play: Proportioned as one’s thought would wish a man—

199.play: And then to have a wretched puling fool,

200.play: A whining mammet, in her fortune’s tender,
notes: mammet] NARES. A puppet, or doll; a diminutive of mam. ‘Quasi dicat parvam matrem, seu matronulam.’—Minshew. ‘Mammetts, puppets, icunculæ.’—Coles. ‘Icunculæ—mammetes or puppets that goe by devises of wyer or strings, as though they had life and moving.’—Abr. Fleming's Nomencl., p. 308. [SING.] It has been supposed to be a corruption of monument. Often used as a jocular term of reproach to young women [this passage cited]. It was sometimes written maumet. Holinshed also speaks of ‘mawmets and idols.’—Hist. of Engl., p. 108. Ruddiman, in the Glossary to Douglas’s Virgil, favours the derivation from Mahomet in mammentis. DYCE. That mammet here means ‘puppet’ (used as a term of reproach) is certain. CLARKE. In Archbishop Trench's admirable book ‘On the Study of Words,’ he traces the origin of this word to ‘Mahomet;’ because the religion of the Arabian prophet was synonymous, in the minds of English Christians, with idolatry, it being forgotten that the most characteristic feature and chief glory of Mahometanism is its protest against all idol-worship whatever. From this original error and injustice arose the habit of applying the word ‘mammet’ (a corruption of ‘Mahomet’) not only to idols or religious images, but to dolls and puppets. [The substance of Trench’s remarks is to be found in the Var. notes on 1 Hen. IV: II, iii, 95.] ED.
her fortune's tender] CLARKE. ‘In the moment when good fortune presents itself to her.’

201.play: To answer “I’ll not wed. I cannot love.

202.play: I am too young. I pray you, pardon me.”

203.play: But, an you will not wed, I’ll pardon you!

204.play: Graze where you will, you shall not house with me.

205.play: Look to ’t; think on ’t. I do not use to jest.

206.play: Thursday is near. Lay hand on heart; advise.

207.play: An you be mine, I’ll give you to my friend.

208.play: An you be not, hang, beg, starve, die in the streets,

209.play: For, by my soul, I’ll ne’er acknowledge thee,

210.play: Nor what is mine shall never do thee good.

211.play: Trust to ’t; bethink you. I’ll not be forsworn. He exits.

212.play: Is there no pity sitting in the clouds

213.play: That sees into the bottom of my grief?—

214.play: O sweet my mother, cast me not away.

215.play: Delay this marriage for a month, a week,

216.play: Or, if you do not, make the bridal bed

217.play: In that dim monument where Tybalt lies.

218.play: Talk not to me, for I’ll not speak a word.

219.play: Do as thou wilt, for I have done with thee. She exits.

221.play: O God! O nurse, how shall this be prevented?

222.play: My husband is on Earth, my faith in heaven.

223.play: How shall that faith return again to Earth

224.play: Unless that husband send it me from heaven

225.play: By leaving Earth? Comfort me; counsel me.—

226.play: Alack, alack, that heaven should practice stratagems

227.play: Upon so soft a subject as myself.—

228.play: What sayst thou? Hast thou not a word of joy?

notes: Some comfort] COLL. (ed. 1). This is also one of the parts of (Q1) which reads as if it had been made up of imperfect notes. WHITE. For this impassioned speech the (Q1) has but a single line. But this line is redundant and plainly corrupt, and contains the two words of the perfect speech which would be most likely to impress a hearer, and which are necessary to carry on the dialogue. The deficiency, and the other wide difference between the two texts just here, I believe to be owing to the surreptitious manner in which the earlier was obtained, and the haste with which it was printed.

Faith, here] STEEV. The character of the Nurse exhibits a just picture of those whose actions have no principles for their foundation. She has been unfaithful to the trust reposed in her by Capulet, and is ready to embrace any expedient that offers to avert the consequences of her first infidelity. [Sing. Verp. Huds. MAL. This picture, however, is not an original. In Romeus and Juliet the Nurse exhibits the same readiness to accommodate herself to the present conjuncture. [Sing. Verp. Huds. BLACKSTONE. Sir John Vanbrugh, in The Relapse, has copied, in this respect, the character of his Nurse from Sh. [Sing. Verp. Huds.

231.play: Romeo is banished, and all the world to nothing

232.play: That he dares ne’er come back to challenge you,

233.play: Or, if he do, it needs must be by stealth.

234.play: Then, since the case so stands as now it doth,

235.play: I think it best you married with the County.

237.play: Romeo’s a dishclout to him. An eagle, madam,

238.play: Hath not so green, so quick, so fair an eye
notes: not so green] STEEV. Perhaps Chaucer has given to Emetrius, in The Knight’s Tale, eyes of the same colour: ‘His nose was high, his eyin bright citryn;’ i.e., the hue of an unripe lemon or citron. Again, in The Two Noble Kinsmen, by Fletcher and Sh., V, i: ‘ —— oh vouchsafe With that thy rare green eye,’ &c. [HudS.] I may add that Arthur Hall (the most ignorant and absurd of all the translators of Homer), in the fourth Iliad, 1581, calls Minerva ‘The greene eide Goddesse.’ [Sing.] DOUCE. Besides the authorities already produced in favor of green eyes, and which show the impropriety of Hanmer’s alteration to keen, a hundred others might, if necessary, be given. The early French poets are extremely fond of alluding to them under the title of yeux vers, which Mons. Le Grand has in vain attempted to convert into yeux vaïrs, or grey eyes. It must be confessed that the scarcity, if not total absence, of such eyes in modern times might well have excited the doubts of the above intelligent and agreeable writer. For this let naturalists, if they can, account. It is certain that green eyes were found among the ancients. Plautus thus alludes to them in his Curculio: ‘Qui hic est homo Cum collativo ventre, atque oculis herbeis?’ Lord Verulam says, ‘Great eyes with a green circle between the white and the white of the eye signify long life.’—Hist. of Life and Death, p. 124. Villa Real, a Portuguese, has written a treatise in praise of them, and they are even said to exist now among his countrymen. See Pinkerton’s Geography, vol. i, p. 556. [Sing. Hal.] COLL. (ed. 2). These citations unquestionably establish the point. HUDS. Lord Bacon says that 'eyes somewhat large, and the circles of them inclined to greenness, are signs of long life.' [Clarke.] DYCE. ‘Green eyes were considered as peculiarly beautiful. . . . The Spanish writers are peculiarly enthusiastic in the praise of green eyes. So Cervantes, in his novel El Zeloso Estremeño: “Ay que ojos tan grandes y tan rasgados! y por el siglo de mi madre, que son verdes, que no parecen sino que son de esmeraldas.”’ (WEBER). Gifford, after observing that he has ‘seen many Norwegian seamen with eyes of this hue, which were invariably quick, keen, and glancing,’ and that the expression ‘green eyes’ is common in our early poets, cites the following Sonnet by Drummond of Hawthmden:

239.play: As Paris hath. Beshrew my very heart,

240.play: I think you are happy in this second match,

241.play: For it excels your first, or, if it did not,

242.play: Your first is dead, or ’twere as good he were

243.play: As living here and you no use of him.

245.play: And from my soul too, else beshrew them both.

248.play: Well, thou hast comforted me marvelous much.

249.play: Go in and tell my lady I am gone,

250.play: Having displeased my father, to Lawrence’ cell

251.play: To make confession and to be absolved.

252.play: Marry, I will; and this is wisely done.     She exits.

253.play: Ancient damnation, O most wicked fiend!

254.play: Is it more sin to wish me thus forsworn

255.play: Or to dispraise my lord with that same tongue

256.play: Which she hath praised him with above compare

257.play: So many thousand times? Go, counselor.

258.play: Thou and my bosom henceforth shall be twain.

259.play: I’ll to the Friar to know his remedy.

260.play: If all else fail, myself have power to die. She exits.

2.play: On Thursday, sir? The time is very short.

3.play: My father Capulet will have it so,

4.play: And I am nothing slow to slack his haste.
notes: so as to slack his haste.] SING. Sh. has hastily fallen into similar inadvertencies elsewhere.KNT. The meaning is obvious as it stands: ‘I am nothing slow (so as), to slack his haste.’STA. Sh.’s marvellous power of condensation sometimes renders his meaning obscure. In this instance, the sense appears to be, ‘and I am not slow in my own preparations for the wedding, to give him any reason to slacken his hasty proceedings.’ [Dyce (ed. 2).COLL. (ed. 2). We should rather say, ‘I am something slow,’ &c.; and what Paris means, obviously, is, I have no wish that he should lessen his haste. The (Q1) makes the speech the very reverse.KTLY. Collier’s (MS.) mistakes the sense. ‘To’ is so as to, that I should. Editors have not understood it.CLARKE. There are remarkably few instances of elliptical diction in the present play. It is a form that Sh. used but sparingly in his earlier dramas, whereas, in his latter ones, it occurs perpetually. As his habit of writing and facility of expression increased, so his power of condensed and inclusive phraseology strengthened; while his own taste and judgment made him ever more and more exercise it as a skill in itself and productive of the most vigorous effect.

5.play: You say you do not know the lady’s mind?

6.play: Uneven is the course. I like it not.

7.play: Immoderately she weeps for Tybalt’s death,

8.play: And therefore have I little talk of love,
notes: talk’d] MOMMSEN. By ‘talk’d’ the meaning is wholly changed. Paris does not here wish to give to the Friar, as an excuse for his uncertainty concerning Juliet’s mind, that, owing to her grief for Tybalt, he had been unable to talk befittingly with her about love, but he simply explains, by this grief, Juliet’s silence and reserve in his own favour; this was the only reason why he received from her so few words of love. Since this interpretation gives throughout a clear meaning—for that Paris does not positively know how Juliet is minded does not preclude the conviction on his part that the expression of her love is alone wanting,—since it renders more graceful the connection with what follows, in so far as Julia, silent about love, is his sorrowing Venus, and since I have talk could have been more easily corrupted into I have talk’d than the reverse, we abide by the old reading.

9.play: For Venus smiles not in a house of tears.

10.play: Now, sir, her father counts it dangerous

11.play: That she do give her sorrow so much sway,
notes: so much sway] COLL. (ed. 2). There seems much reason in the emendation of the (MS.).

12.play: And in his wisdom hastes our marriage

13.play: To stop the inundation of her tears,

14.play: Which, too much minded by herself alone,

15.play: May be put from her by society.

16.play: Now do you know the reason of this haste.

17.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE, aside

18.play: I would I knew not why it should be slowed.—

19.play: Look, sir, here comes the lady toward my cell.

20.play: [Enter Juliet.]

21.play: Happily met, my lady and my wife.

22.play: That may be, sir, when I may be a wife.

23.play: That “may be” must be, love, on Thursday next.

24.play: What must be shall be.

25.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: That’s a certain text.

26.play: Come you to make confession to this father?

27.play: To answer that, I should confess to you.

28.play: Do not deny to him that you love me.

29.play: I will confess to you that I love him.

30.play: So will you, I am sure, that you love me.

31.play: If I do so, it will be of more price

32.play: Being spoke behind your back than to your face.

33.play: Poor soul, thy face is much abused with tears.

34.play: The tears have got small victory by that,

35.play: For it was bad enough before their spite.

36.play: Thou wrong’st it more than tears with that report.

37.play: That is no slander, sir, which is a truth,

38.play: And what I spake, I spake it to my face.

39.play: Thy face is mine, and thou hast slandered it.

40.play: It may be so, for it is not mine own.—

41.play: Are you at leisure, holy father, now,

42.play: Or shall I come to you at evening Mass?
notes: evening mass] Ritson. Juliet means vespers. There is no such thing as evening mass. [Huds. White.] ‘Masses,’ as Fynes Moryson observes, ‘are only sung in the morning, and when the priests are fasting.’ [Sing.] So, likewise, in The Boke of Thenseygnementes and Techynge that the Knight of the Toure made to his Doughters, translated and printed by Caxton: ‘And they of the parryshe told the preest that it was past none, and therefor he durst not synge masse, and so they hadde no masse that daye.’ [Hal. Sta. It is strange that Sh., who on other occasions has shown a competent knowledge of the doctrines and usages of the Roman Catholic Church, should have fallen into this error. The celebration of mass, as is well known, can only take place in the forenoon. Clarke. The word ‘mass’ is here employed in the general sense of ‘service,’ ‘office,’ ‘prayer;’ while, on the contrary, the Italians usually apply their word funzione to ‘high mass’ only, though in strictness it means ‘divine service’ generally.

43.play: My leisure serves me, pensive daughter, now.—

44.play: My lord, we must entreat the time alone.

45.play: God shield I should disturb devotion!—

46.play: Juliet, on Thursday early will I rouse you.

47.play: Till then, adieu, and keep this holy kiss.  He exits.

48.play: O, shut the door, and when thou hast done so,

49.play: Come weep with me, past hope, past care, past help.
notes: past cure] Del. So in Love’s Lab. L., V, ii, 28. Ulr. This change from care to cure is not only needless, but even objectionable. Past cure is the same as past help, and therefore only a weak repetition of the same thought. ‘Past all hope, past all care or effort (for escape), past all help,’ perfectly expresses the desperate position and mood in which Juliet finds herself.

50.play: O Juliet, I already know thy grief.

51.play: It strains me past the compass of my wits.

52.play: I hear thou must, and nothing may prorogue it,

53.play: On Thursday next be married to this County.

54.play: Tell me not, friar, that thou hearest of this,

55.play: Unless thou tell me how I may prevent it.

56.play: If in thy wisdom thou canst give no help,

57.play: Do thou but call my resolution wise,

58.play: And with this knife I’ll help it presently. She shows him her knife.
notes: this knife] WHITE. The ladies of Sh.'s day customarily wore knives at their girdles.

59.play: God joined my heart and Romeo’s, thou our hands;

60.play: And ere this hand, by thee to Romeo’s sealed,

61.play: Shall be the label to another deed,
notes: the label] MAL. The seals of deeds in our author's time were not impressed on the parchment itself on which the deed was written, but were appended on distinct slips or labels affixed to the deed. Hence, in Rich. II: V, ii, 56, the Duke of York discovers, by the depending seal, a covenant into which his son, the Duke of Aumerle, had entered: 'What seal is that which hangs without thy bosom?' [SING. CORN. VERP. HUDS. STA. DYCE, CLARKE, HAL.]

62.play: Or my true heart with treacherous revolt

63.play: Turn to another, this shall slay them both.

64.play: Therefore out of thy long-experienced time
notes: Therefore out of] This line is cited by S. WALKER ('Crit.' vol. ii, p. 173) as an instance of the peculiar accentuation of the preposition 'of.'

65.play: Give me some present counsel, or, behold,

66.play: ’Twixt my extremes and me this bloody knife

67.play: Shall play the umpire, arbitrating that
notes: the umpire] JOHNSON. That is, this knife shall decide the struggle between me and my distresses. [SING.

68.play: Which the commission of thy years and art
notes: the commission] JOHNSON. Commission is for authority or power. [SING. ULR. I do not think that commission stands here, as Johnson says, for 'authority,' or 'power,' but is used in its ordinary sense. Juliet says in effect: this knife shall decide that which the commission [die Vollmacht] that thy age and thy art give thee—the commission, namely, to appoint an umpire—could not bring to an honourable issue.

69.play: Could to no issue of true honor bring.

70.play: Be not so long to speak. I long to die
notes: Be not so long] CLARKE. The constraint, with sparing speech, visible in Juliet when with her parents, as contrasted with her free outpouring flow of words when she is with her lover, her father-confessor, or her nurse—when, in short, she is her natural self and at perfect ease—is true to characteristic delineation.
