The young girl, the very young girl, the girl brought up as Juliet has been reared, the youthful southern maiden, lives and breathes in every line by which Sh. has set her before us.

71.play: If what thou speak’st speak not of remedy.

72.play: Hold, daughter, I do spy a kind of hope,

73.play: Which craves as desperate an execution
notes: as desperate] Clarke. It is interesting to observe how different is the style here, in one of Sh.'s earlier written plays, from the style in his later ones. The repetition of the word 'desprate,' the precision of statement in this comparison, is utterly contrary to the conciseness, the elliptical condensedness, which we find in the comparisons from Sh.'s hand at a later date.
an execution] S. Walker. I suspect an is an interpolation. (Vol. i, p. 269, Art. xl: 'Metre affeecd by the pronunciation of ion final.')

74.play: As that is desperate which we would prevent.

75.play: If, rather than to marry County Paris,

76.play: Thou hast the strength of will to slay thyself,

77.play: Then is it likely thou wilt undertake

78.play: A thing like death to chide away this shame,

79.play: That cop’st with death himself to ’scape from it;

80.play: And if thou darest, I’ll give thee remedy.
notes: And if] Dfl. According to the punctuation of (Q,) Qq, which puts a stop at the end of the preceding sentence, 'And if' should here be read as 'An if.'

81.play: O, bid me leap, rather than marry Paris,

82.play: From off the battlements of any tower,
notes: yonder tower] Ulr. But I cannot perceive why Juliet must designate a particular, actual tower, since all that follows is purely imaginary, the tasks of horror which her imagination conjured up. And besides, the expression, 'Bid me leap from any (no matter how high) tower' is more vigorous than 'from that tower there.' White. 'Yonder' has been almost universally followed hitherto as the more poetic reading. But the passage was evidently rewritten on the revision of the play, as will be seen by comparison with the earliest text, which will give the reader a fair notion of the nature and extent of the variations between the two versions in this part of the play, all of which cannot be noticed. It is difficult to see why one word of the revised version should be rejected while all the others are accepted.

83.play: Or walk in thievish ways, or bid me lurk

84.play: Where serpents are. Chain me with roaring bears,

85.play: Or hide me nightly in a charnel house,

86.play: O’ercovered quite with dead men’s rattling bones,

87.play: With reeky shanks and yellow chapless skulls.

88.play: Or bid me go into a new-made grave

89.play: And hide me with a dead man in his shroud

90.play: (Things that to hear them told have made me tremble),

91.play: And I will do it without fear or doubt,

92.play: To live an unstained wife to my sweet love.

93.play: Hold, then. Go home; be merry; give consent

94.play: To marry Paris. Wednesday is tomorrow.

95.play: Tomorrow night look that thou lie alone;

96.play: Let not the Nurse lie with thee in thy chamber. Holding out a vial.

97.play: Take thou this vial, being then in bed,

98.play: And this distilling liquor drink thou off;

99.play: When presently through all thy veins shall run

100.play: A cold and drowsy humor; for no pulse

101.play: Shall keep his native progress, but surcease.

102.play: No warmth, no breath shall testify thou livest.

103.play: The roses in thy lips and cheeks shall fade

104.play: To paly ashes, thy eyes’ windows fall

105.play: Like death when he shuts up the day of life.

106.play: Each part, deprived of supple government,

107.play: Shall, stiff and stark and cold, appear like death,

108.play: And in this borrowed likeness of shrunk death

109.play: Thou shalt continue two and forty hours

110.play: And then awake as from a pleasant sleep.

111.play: Now, when the bridegroom in the morning comes

112.play: To rouse thee from thy bed, there art thou dead.

113.play: Then, as the manner of our country is,

114.play: In thy best robes uncovered on the bier
notes: on the bier] KNT. The editors [in omitting the line from QqFf] have here gone far beyond their office; nor can we understand why the more particular working out of the idea in the next two lines should have given them offence. 'Be borne' means 'to be borne.' DYCE ('Remarks,' &c., p. 174). The line [of the QqFf] is a various lection of the two lines. I apprehend that Knight would search the poetry of England in vain for another example of such an ellipsis as 'Be borne' for to be borne. When Beaumont and Fletcher imitated the passage in The Knight of Malta, IV, i, they were content with one reading. LETTSOM [marginal MS. note on the above in the present editor's copy]. Very true. These various lections, like those in Love’s Lab. L., seem to have originated in transcribing from Sh.'s foul copies. ULr. The hypothesis that the line 'Be borne,' &c., retained its place in the MS. only through an oversight of Sh. when he revised the piece (about 1598), supposes that the printer of Qq had before him Sh.'s own handwriting, which is very improbable. At all events, it is unscholarly upon such an hypothesis to omit the line altogether. For although it is not to be denied that it seems superfluous, yet it may be quite easily conformed to the construction, if Knight's explanation of the ellipsis be correct. CAMBR. We have [here] omitted a line which occurs in all the Quartos, except the first, and all the Folios, because it could not be retained without absolute detriment to the sense.

115.play: Thou shalt be borne to that same ancient vault

116.play: Where all the kindred of the Capulets lie.

117.play: In the meantime, against thou shalt awake,

118.play: Shall Romeo by my letters know our drift,

119.play: And hither shall he come, and he and I

120.play: Will watch thy waking, and that very night

121.play: Shall Romeo bear thee hence to Mantua.

122.play: And this shall free thee from this present shame,

123.play: If no inconstant toy nor womanish fear
notes: inconstant toy] JOHNSON. If no fickle freak, no light caprice, no change of fancy, hinder the performance. [Sing. MAL. These expressions ['inconstant toy' and 'womanish fear'] are borrowed from the poem. [Sing.

124.play: Abate thy valor in the acting it.

125.play: Give me, give me! O, tell not me of fear!
notes: Give me] DYCE (ed. 2). Probably the modern alteration, 'Give me, O, give me! tell,' &c., is what the poet wrote. I believe that the 'it' [of Lettson's conj.] is unnecessary here. Compare Macbeth, I, iii, 5: '"Give me," quoth I.

126.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE, giving Juliet the vial

127.play: Hold, get you gone. Be strong and prosperous

128.play: In this resolve. I’ll send a friar with speed

129.play: To Mantua with my letters to thy lord.

130.play: Love give me strength, and strength shall help afford.

131.play: Farewell, dear father. They exit in different directions.

1.play: [Enter Father Capulet, Mother, Nurse, and Servingmen, two or three.]

2.play: So many guests invite as here are writ. One or two of the Servingmen exit with Capulet’s list.

3.play: Sirrah, go hire me twenty cunning cooks.
notes: twenty cunning cooks] RITSON. Twenty cooks for half a dozen guests! Either Capulet has altered his mind strangely, or Sh. forgot what he had just made him tell us. [III, iv, 27.] [Sing. Dyce, Hal. MAL. This arose from his sometimes following and sometimes deserting his original. The scene referred to was his own invention; but here he recollected the poem: '-- he myndes to make for him a costly feast.' [Sing. Dyce, Hal. KNT. According to an entry in the books of the Stationers' Company for 1560, the preacher was paid six shillings and twopence for his labour; the minstrel, twelve

4.play: SERVINGMAN: You shall have none ill, sir, for I’ll try if they can lick their fingers.

5.play: CAPULET: How canst thou try them so?

6.play: SERVINGMAN: Marry, sir, ’tis an ill cook that cannot lick his own fingers. Therefore he that cannot lick his fingers goes not with me.

7.play: CAPULET: Go, begone.   Servingman exits.

8.play: We shall be much unfurnished for this time.—

9.play: What, is my daughter gone to Friar Lawrence?

10.play: NURSE: Ay, forsooth.

11.play: Well, he may chance to do some good on her.

12.play: A peevish self-willed harlotry it is.
notes: harlotry] DEL. Sh. has also elsewhere used this abstract for the concrete not only in its own proper signification, but also in a forced meaning as a term of reproach; thus, and with the same adjective as here, in 1 Hen. IV: III, i, 198, spoken of the headstrong Lady Mortimer. COLL. (ed. 2). It is used both as an adjective and substantive. In 1 Hen. IV: II, iv, 436, Mrs. Quickly speaks of 'these harlotry players.' WHITE. [Note on 1 Hen. IV: III, i, 198]. This phrase was used with as little meaning of reproach in Elizabeth's time as 'slut' was in Queen Anne's, or as Lady Percy implies in calling her restive husband 'thief.'

13.play: [Enter Juliet.]

14.play: See where she comes from shrift with merry look.

15.play: How now, my headstrong, where have you been gadding?
notes: gadding] STEEV. The primitive sense of this word was to straggle from house to house, and collect money, under pretence of singing carols to the Blessed Virgin. See T. Warton's note on Milton's Lycidas, v. 40. DOUCE. Steevens's derivation seems too refined. Warton's authority is an old register at Gadderston, in these words: 'Receyvid at the gadryng with Saynte Mary songe at Crismas.' If the original were attentively examined, it would perhaps turn out that the word in question has some mark of contraction over it, which would convert it into gaderyngs—i. e., gathering or collecting money, and not simply going about from house to house, according to Warton's explanation.

16.play: Where I have learned me to repent the sin

17.play: Of disobedient opposition

18.play: To you and your behests, and am enjoined

19.play: By holy Lawrence to fall prostrate here     Kneeling.
notes: prostrate here] WHITE. The scene as it stands in (Q1) I believe to have been chiefly supplied from memory by some inferior versifier employed by the publisher.

20.play: To beg your pardon. Pardon, I beseech you.

21.play: Henceforward I am ever ruled by you.

22.play: Send for the County. Go tell him of this.

23.play: I’ll have this knot knit up tomorrow morning.

24.play: I met the youthful lord at Lawrence’ cell

25.play: And gave him what becomèd love I might,
notes: become] STEEV. For becoming; one participle for another,—a frequent practice in Sh.'s day. [Sing. Huds. White. DKL. That is, such love as was befitting. It is not precisely the same as ‘becoming love,’ which means such love as is befitting.

26.play: Not stepping o’er the bounds of modesty.

27.play: Why, I am glad on ’t. This is well. Stand up. Juliet rises.

28.play: This is as ’t should be.—Let me see the County.

29.play: Ay, marry, go, I say, and fetch him hither.—

30.play: Now, afore God, this reverend holy friar,

31.play: All our whole city is much bound to him.

32.play: Nurse, will you go with me into my closet

33.play: To help me sort such needful ornaments

34.play: As you think fit to furnish me tomorrow?

35.play: No, not till Thursday. There is time enough.

36.play: Go, nurse. Go with her. We’ll to church tomorrow. Juliet and the Nurse exit.

37.play: We shall be short in our provision.

38.play: ’Tis now near night.
notes: near night] MAL. In III, v, Romeo parted from his bride at daybreak on Tuesday morning. Immediately afterwards she went to Friar Laurence, and he particularly mentions (IV, i, 90) that the next day is Wednesday. She could not well have remained more than an hour or two with the Friar, and she is just now returned from shrift; yet Lady Capulet says, ‘’Tis near night,’ and this same night is ascertained to be Tuesday. This is one of the many instances of Sh.’s inaccuracy in the computation of time. ULR. Malone is perfectly right, and would never have made such a mistake;—but Sh., marry, was no Malone. CLARKE. If the indications of time be examined in the present play, we shall see how ingeniously Sh. has taken pains to trace it all along. In Scene i, the Prince ```

39.play: CAPULET: Tush, I will stir about,

40.play: And all things shall be well, I warrant thee, wife.

41.play: Go thou to Juliet. Help to deck up her.
notes: up her] DYCE (ed. 2). ‘Should not the preposition come last [as in "prepare him up," line 45, and "trim her up," IV, iv, 25], the pronoun not being emphatic?’—W. N. LETTSOM.

42.play: I’ll not to bed tonight. Let me alone.

43.play: I’ll play the housewife for this once.—What ho!—

44.play: They are all forth. Well, I will walk myself

45.play: To County Paris, to prepare up him
notes: him up] DEL. The Ff yield the better reading. The pronoun is not emphatic.

46.play: Against tomorrow. My heart is wondrous light

47.play: Since this same wayward girl is so reclaimed. They exit.

2.play: Ay, those attires are best. But, gentle nurse,

3.play: I pray thee leave me to myself tonight,

4.play: For I have need of many orisons

5.play: To move the heavens to smile upon my state,

6.play: Which, well thou knowest, is cross and full of sin.

7.play: [Enter Lady Capulet.]

8.play: What, are you busy, ho? Need you my help?

9.play: No, madam, we have culled such necessaries

10.play: As are behooveful for our state tomorrow.

11.play: So please you, let me now be left alone,

12.play: And let the Nurse this night sit up with you,

13.play: For I am sure you have your hands full all

14.play: In this so sudden business.

15.play: LADY CAPULET: Good night.

16.play: Get thee to bed and rest, for thou hast need. Lady Capulet and the Nurse exit.

17.play: Farewell.—God knows when we shall meet again.

18.play: I have a faint cold fear thrills through my veins
notes: cold fear thrills] MAL. So in Romeus and Juliet: 'Her dainty tender partes gan shever all for dred, Her golden heares did stand upright upon her chillish hed. Then pressed with the feare that she there lived in, A sweat as colde as mountaine yse pearst through her slender skin.' Sla.

19.play: That almost freezes up the heat of life.

20.play: I’ll call them back again to comfort me.—

21.play: Nurse!—What should she do here?

22.play: My dismal scene I needs must act alone.

23.play: Come, vial.     She takes out the vial.

24.play: What if this mixture do not work at all?

25.play: Shall I be married then tomorrow morning? She takes out her knife and puts it down beside her.
notes: to-morrow morning] KNT. This speech, like many others of the great passages throughout the play, 'received the most careful elaboration and the most minute touching. DYCE pronounces this line much more 'tame' than that from (Q,).

26.play: No, no, this shall forbid it. Lie thou there.
notes: Lie thou there] STEEV. It appears, from several passages in our old plays, that knives were formerly part of the accoutrements of a bride, and everything behoveful for Juliet's state had just been left with her. So in Decker's Match Me in London, 1631: 'See at my girdle hang my wedding knives!' Again, in King Edward III, 1599: 'Here at my side do hang my wedding knives.' Again: ' — there was a maid named, &c.—She tooke one of her knives that was some halfe a foote long,' &c. &c. 'And it was found in all respects like to the other that was in her sheath.'—Goulart's Admirable Histories, 1607, pp. 176, 178. In Sidney's Arcadia, b. iii, we are likewise informed that Amphialus 'in his crest carried Philoclea's knives, the only token of her forced favour.' [Hal. MAL. In order to account for Juliet's having a dagger, it is not necessary to have recourse to the ancient accoutrements of brides, how prevalent soever the custom may have been; for Juliet appears to have furnished herself with this instrument immediately after her father and mother had threatened to force her to marry Paris: 'If all else fail, myself have power to die.' Accordingly, in the very next scene, when she is at the Friar's cell, and before she could have been furnished with any of the apparatus of a bride (not having then consented to marry the count), she says: "'Twixt me and my extremes this bloody knife shall," &c. [Hal. BOSWELL. Gifford, in a note on Jonson's Staple of News, informs us that in Sh.'s time 'daggers, or, as they were more commonly called, knives, were worn at all times by every woman in England.' [SING. finishes the sentence]: 'Whether they were so worn in Italy, Sh., I believe, never inquired, and I cannot tell.' [Coll. Verp. Hudsp. Hal. COLL. (ed. 2). It certainly was the case. DYCE. (ed. 2). 'The omission of "knife" is peculiarly awkward, as Juliet has been addressing the vial just before.'—W. N. LETTSOM.

27.play: What if it be a poison which the Friar

28.play: Subtly hath ministered to have me dead,

29.play: Lest in this marriage he should be dishonored

30.play: Because he married me before to Romeo?

31.play: I fear it is. And yet methinks it should not,

32.play: For he hath still been tried a holy man.
notes: holy man] COLL. The line adopted by Steevens from (Q₁) seems necessary to the completeness of the rejection of Juliet’s suspicion of the Friar. [Verp. ULR. If it be assumed that Juliet, or rather Sh., wishes to thrust aside utterly the suspicion which comes up in her mind, then this line is absolutely necessary. But it may fairly be asked whether this were the intention of the poet. It was emphatically so according to the text of (Q₁). On the other hand, the enlarging and revising which the whole monologue received in the ‘corrected, augmented, and amended’ edition of Q₂ consists precisely herein that Sh. brings forward far more strongly and impressively than in (Q₁) the doubts, the apprehensions, and horror which seize Juliet’s soul at the sight of the vial which she must drain, and this is done manifestly to place in clearer light the loftiness of her resolve and the depth of her love and fidelity. With this in view it would clearly be very little to the purpose to represent the suspicion aroused against Laurence as wholly allayed. On the contrary, it must remain, even if it amounts to only a dubious apprehension. WHITE. There is no necessity which justifies the resumption of the line from (Q₁). CLARKE. This line from (Q₁) seems to us so characteristic of Juliet in its sweet, girlish simplicity and trustfulness that we believe it to have been what Sh. wrote and intended to retain, and that it was omitted by mistake in Q₂Ff.

33.play: How if, when I am laid into the tomb,

34.play: I wake before the time that Romeo

35.play: Come to redeem me? There’s a fearful point.

36.play: Shall I not then be stifled in the vault,

37.play: To whose foul mouth no healthsome air breathes in,

38.play: And there die strangled ere my Romeo comes?

39.play: Or, if I live, is it not very like

40.play: The horrible conceit of death and night,
notes: conceit] DEL. That is, the effect which Death and Night in the vaults of the Capulets would have upon Juliet’s imagination.

41.play: Together with the terror of the place—

42.play: As in a vault, an ancient receptacle
notes: As in a vault] STEEV. This idea was probably suggested to Sh. by his native place. The charnel at Stafford-upon-Avon is a very large one, and perhaps contains a greater number of bones than are to be found in any other repository of the same kind in England. [Sing. Knt. Verp. Huds.

43.play: Where for this many hundred years the bones

44.play: Of all my buried ancestors are packed;

45.play: Where bloody Tybalt, yet but green in earth,

46.play: Lies fest’ring in his shroud; where, as they say,
notes: Lies festering] Steev. To fester is to corrupt. So, in King Edward III, 1599: ‘Lilies that fester smell far worse than weeds.’ This line likewise occurs in the 94th Sonnet of Sh. The play of Edward III has been ascribed to him. [Sing.

47.play: At some hours in the night spirits resort—

48.play: Alack, alack, is it not like that I,
notes: is it not like] Del. This repeats the previous question, ‘Is it not very like,’ without completing the sentence to which ‘the horrible conceit’ is the subject—a Shakespearian anacoluthon which here marks Juliet’s excitement.

49.play: So early waking, what with loathsome smells,

50.play: And shrieks like mandrakes torn out of the earth,
notes: mandrakes] Steev. The mandrake (says Thomas Newton in his Herball of the Bible, 1587) has been idly represented as ‘a creature having life and engendered under the earth of the seed of some dead person, who hath beene convicted and put to death for some felonie or murther; and that they had the same in such dampish and funerall places, where the said convicted persons were buried,’ &c. [Sing. Hudss. Ital. Clarke.] In Webster’s Duchess of Malfy, 1623: ‘I have this night dug up a mandrake, And am grown mad with it.’ Again, in the Atheist’s Tragedy, 1611: ‘The cries of mandrakes never touch’d the ear With more sad horror.’ In A Christian turn’d Turk, 1612: ‘I’ll rather give an ear to the black shrieks Of mandrakes,’ &c. In Aristippus or the Jovial Philosopher: ‘This is the mandrake’s voice that undoes me.’ [Hal. Nares. The English name of Mandragoras. An inferior degree of animal life was attributed to it, and it was commonly supposed that when torn from the ground it uttered groans of so pernicious a nature that the person who committed the violence went mad or died. To escape that danger it was recommended to tie one end of a string to the plant and the other to a dog, upon whom the fatal groan would then discharge its full malignity. See Bulleine’s Bulwarke of Defence against Sicknesse, p. 41. These strange notions arose, probably, from the little less fanciful comparison of the root to the human figure, strengthened, doubtless, in England by the accidental circumstance of man being the first syllable of the word. The ancients, however, made the same comparison of its form: ‘Quamvis semihominis, vesano gramine fœta, Mandragoræ pariat flores.’—Columella, de l. Hort., v, 19. The white mandrake, which they called the male, was that whose root bore this resemblance. Lyte says of it, ‘The roote is great and white, not muche unlyke a radishe roote divided into two or three partes and sometimes growing one upon another, almost lyke the legges and thighes of a man.’—Transl. of Dodoens, p. 437. It is supposed to cause death, in 2 Hen. VI: III, ii, 310. A very diminutive or grotesque figure was often compared to a mandrake; that is, to the root, as above described. So in 2 Hen. IV: I, ii, 17. It was sometimes considered as an emblem

51.play: That living mortals, hearing them, run mad—

52.play: O, if I wake, shall I not be distraught,
notes: distraught] STEEV. i. e., distracted. [SING. CLARKE.

53.play: Environèd with all these hideous fears,

54.play: And madly play with my forefathers’ joints,

55.play: And pluck the mangled Tybalt from his shroud,

56.play: And, in this rage, with some great kinsman’s bone,
notes: great kinsman's bone] DEL. This is compounded, like great-nephew, great-grandfather and the like.

57.play: As with a club, dash out my desp’rate brains?

58.play: O look, methinks I see my cousin’s ghost

59.play: Seeking out Romeo that did spit his body

60.play: Upon a rapier’s point! Stay, Tybalt, stay!
notes: stay, Tybalt, stay!] DEL. She does not call upon Tybalt to remain, but to hold. In her vision she imagines that he is going to hurt her lover Romeo.

61.play: Romeo, Romeo, Romeo! Here’s drink. I drink to thee.    She drinks and falls upon her bed within the curtains.
notes: Romeo, I come] DYCE ('Remarks,' &c., p. 175). The line in Q1Ff is partly composed of a stage-direction, 'I'ere drink' having evidently crept into the

2.play: Hold, take these keys, and fetch more spices, nurse.

3.play: They call for dates and quinces in the pastry.
notes: pastry] MAL. That is, in the room where paste was made. So laundry, spicery, &c. [SING.: Coll. Huds. Sta. Cham.] DYC. ‘A Pastry, pistrina, placentaria.’—Coles’s Lat. and Eng. Dict. WHITE. That is, in the place where paste, which we now incorrectly call pastry, is made. ‘Pastry,’ meaning a place, is analogous with ‘dairy,’ ‘aviary,’ ‘buttery,’ &c., &c. CLARKE. Just as ‘pantry’ was the name given to the room where bread (Latin, panis) in former times was exclusively kept; and ‘laundry’ to the one where washing (old French, lavanderie) was done.

dates] BEISLY. Dr. Moffet, in ‘Health Improvement,’ says of dates: ‘They are usually put into stewed broths, mince-pies and restorative cullices, as though they were of great and wholesome nourishment.’ William Turner does not speak so favorably of them, ‘as they fill the stomach full of wind, and are hurtful to them that are disposed to the tooth-ache. Wherefore our sweete-lipped Londoners and wanton courtiers do not wysely to suffer so many dates to be put into their pyes and other meats, to the great charge of their purses, and to no less undoing of the health of their bodies.’ In Westmacott’s ‘Scripture Herbal’ it is said of dates ‘that astrologers have given them to Mars, perhaps to please the lady Venus with.’ In Sylvester’s ‘Dubartas’ the date and olive are noticed as aiding appetite. Gerarde notices the Quince, and says ‘the marmalad or cotiniat of quinces is good and profitable to strengthen the stomach, that it may retain and keep the meat therein, until it be perfectly digested.’

4.play: [Enter old Capulet.]

5.play: Come, stir, stir, stir! The second cock hath crowed.

6.play: The curfew bell hath rung. ’Tis three o’clock.—
notes: The curfew-bell] NARES. The evening bell,—couvre feu. The origin and purpose of this bell are well known. The original time for ringing it was eight in the evening; and we are told by some writers that in many villages the name is still retained for the evening bell. Brand, in his observations on Bourne's Antiquities, says: 'We retain also a vestige of the old Norman curfew at eight in the evening' (chap. i). In The Merry Devil of Edmonton it is represented as having got

7.play: Look to the baked meats, good Angelica.

8.play: Spare not for cost.

9.play: NURSE: Go, you cot-quean, go,

10.play: Get you to bed. Faith, you’ll be sick tomorrow

11.play: For this night’s watching.

12.play: No, not a whit. What, I have watched ere now

13.play: All night for lesser cause, and ne’er been sick.

14.play: Ay, you have been a mouse-hunt in your time,

15.play: But I will watch you from such watching now. Lady Capulet and Nurse exit.

16.play: A jealous hood, a jealous hood!
notes: A jealous-hood] DEL. Jocosely formed, like womanhood and the like, perhaps also in the double sense of a jealous woman's hood. In the old eds. it is two separate words. ULR. It is a question whether Sh. meant this as a compound word. In all the

17.play: [Enter three or four Servingmen with spits and logs and baskets.]

18.play: Now fellow, What is there?

19.play: Things for the cook, sir, but I know not what.

20.play: Make haste, make haste.    First Servingman exits. Sirrah, fetch drier logs.

21.play: Call Peter. He will show thee where they are.

22.play: I have a head, sir, that will find out logs

23.play: And never trouble Peter for the matter.

24.play: Mass, and well said. A merry whoreson, ha!

25.play: Thou shalt be loggerhead. Second Servingman exits. Good faith, ’tis day.

26.play: The County will be here with music straight, Play music.

27.play: For so he said he would. I hear him near.—

28.play: Nurse!—Wife! What ho!—What, nurse, I say!

29.play: [Enter Nurse.]

30.play: Go waken Juliet. Go and trim her up.

31.play: I’ll go and chat with Paris. Hie, make haste,

32.play: Make haste. The bridegroom he is come already.

33.play: Make haste, I say. He exits.

2.play: Mistress! What, mistress! Juliet!—Fast, I warrant her, she—

3.play: Why, lamb, why, lady! Fie, you slugabed!

4.play: Why, love, I say! Madam! Sweetheart! Why, bride!—

5.play: What, not a word?—You take your pennyworths now.

6.play: Sleep for a week, for the next night, I warrant,

7.play: The County Paris hath set up his rest
notes: set up his rest] STEEV. This expression, frequently employed by the old dramatic writers, is taken from the manner of firing the harquebuss, which was so heavy that a supporter, called a rest, was fixed in the ground before the piece was levelled to take aim. Decker, in Old Fortunatus, 1600: ‘——set your heart at rest, for I have set up my rest, that unless you can run swifter than a hart,’ &c. Also in B. and Fl.’s Elder Brother: ‘—— My rest is up, Nor will I go less.’ Again in the Roaring Girl, 1611: ‘Like a musket on a rest.’ See Montfaucon’s Monarchie Françoise, tom. v, pl. 48. [HAL. REED. It is, however, oftener employed with reference to the game at primero, in which it was one of the terms then in use. In the second instance above quoted it is certainly so. See Dodsley’s Collection of Old Plays, vol. x, p. 364, edit. 1780, where several instances are brought together. [HAL. M. MASON. It means that the gamester has determined what stake he would play for. In the passage from Fletcher’s Elder Brother, where Eustace says, ‘My rest is up, and I will go no less,’ he means to say, my stake is laid, and I will not play for a smaller. The same phrase very frequently occurs in the plays of B. and Fl. It is also used by Lord Clarendon in his History, as well as in the old comedy of Supposes, 1587. [HAL. BOSWELL. Nash quibbles upon this word in his Terrors of the Night: ‘You that are married and have wives of your owne, and yet hold too nere frendship with your neighbours, set up your rests, that the Night will be an ill neighbour to your rest, and that you shall have as little peace of mind as the rest.’ [SING. HAL. NARES. A metaphor from the game of primero, meaning, to stand upon the cards you have in your hand in hopes that they may prove better than your adversary. Hence to make up your mind, to be determined. It is fully explained in an epigram of Sir J. Harington’s, where Marcus, a foolish gamester, is described as standing at first upon small games and consequently losing; but still losing, by the fraud of his antagonists, even when he grew more wary. Hence we may see how erroneous was one of Steevens’ explanations of this phrase. I say one, for he has given the right in other places. A rest was, in fact, an appendage to every matchlock gun, not particularly the harquebuss, because the soldier could not manage his match without it. There was, therefore, such a rest, but that was not the allusion. [STA. substantially. SING. (ed. 1) [Note on All’s Well, II, i, 138]. This word furnished many other proverbial expressions among the Italians, one of which is to be found in the Ciriffo Calvanco of Luca Pulci: ‘Fa del suo resto,’ to adventure all. ‘Haver fatto del resto,’ to have lost all or have nothing to rest upon. ‘Riservar il Resto,’ to reserve one’s rest, to be wary and circumspect, &c., &c. All authorities are decisive upon the derivation of this word from Primero, as Nares has amply shown. . . . In Spanish too, ‘Echar el resto,’ to set or lay up one’s rest, has the same origin and figurative meaning—to adventure all, to be determined. We shall now, it is to be hoped, hear no more of musket rests, &c., in explanation of this phrase. COLL. (ed. 1). A figurative expression apparently derived from the mode of firing

8.play: That you shall rest but little.—God forgive me,

9.play: Marry, and amen! How sound is she asleep!

10.play: I needs must wake her.—Madam, madam, madam!

11.play: Ay, let the County take you in your bed,

12.play: He’ll fright you up, i’ faith.—Will it not be? She opens the bed’s curtains.

13.play: What, dressed, and in your clothes, and down again?

14.play: I must needs wake you. Lady, lady, lady!—

15.play: Alas, alas! Help, help! My lady’s dead.—

16.play: O, weraday, that ever I was born!—

17.play: Some aqua vitae, ho!—My lord! My lady!

18.play: [Enter Lady Capulet.]

19.play: What noise is here?

20.play: NURSE: O lamentable day!

21.play: What is the matter?

22.play: NURSE: Look, look!—O heavy day!

23.play: O me! O me! My child, my only life,

24.play: Revive, look up, or I will die with thee.

25.play: Help, help! Call help.

26.play: [Enter Capulet.]

27.play: For shame, bring Juliet forth. Her lord is come.

28.play: She’s dead, deceased. She’s dead, alack the day!

29.play: Alack the day, she’s dead, she’s dead, she’s dead.

30.play: Ha, let me see her! Out, alas, she’s cold.
notes: let me see her] WHITE. The variations between the earlier and later texts are very great in this scene. The commonplace thoughts and the feeble, formal rhythm of the former, in most of the passages peculiar to it, warrant the belief that they were supplied by another hand than Sh.’s.

31.play: Her blood is settled, and her joints are stiff.

32.play: Life and these lips have long been separated.

33.play: Death lies on her like an untimely frost

34.play: Upon the sweetest flower of all the field.

35.play: O lamentable day!

36.play: LADY CAPULET: O woeful time!

37.play: Death, that hath ta’en her hence to make me wail,

38.play: Ties up my tongue and will not let me speak.
notes: will not let me speak] MAL. SH. has here followed the poem closely,

39.play: [Enter Friar Lawrence and the County Paris, with Musicians.]

40.play: Come, is the bride ready to go to church?

41.play: Ready to go, but never to return.—

42.play: O son, the night before thy wedding day

43.play: Hath Death lain with thy wife. There she lies,

44.play: Flower as she was, deflowerèd by him.

45.play: Death is my son-in-law; Death is my heir.

46.play: My daughter he hath wedded. I will die

47.play: And leave him all. Life, living, all is Death’s.

48.play: Have I thought long to see this morning’s face,
notes: Have I thought] WHITE. After this line, (Q1) has a passage which requires higher authority than that of such a publication to cause it to be received as Sh.’s.

49.play: And doth it give me such a sight as this?

51.play: Most miserable hour that e’er time saw

52.play: In lasting labor of his pilgrimage!
notes: labour] DEL. This word, as applied to the toilsome progress of time, Sh. has again used in Timon, III, iv, 8.

53.play: But one, poor one, one poor and loving child,

54.play: But one thing to rejoice and solace in,

55.play: And cruel death hath catched it from my sight!
notes: from my sight] ULR. [Quotes the stage-direction of (Q1) at the end of this speech, and continues]: If this passage and the whole scene as it stands in (Q1) do not prove that Romeo and Juliet in its earliest shape belongs to the youthful labours of Sh., then all proofs of the date of its origin drawn from the internal and circumstantial evidence of the piece must be entirely discarded.

56.play: O woe, O woeful, woeful, woeful day!
notes: O woe!] WHITE. In this speech of mock heroic woe, and perhaps in the two that follow, Sh. seems to have ridiculed, as he has done elsewhere, the translation of Seneca’s Tragedies, published in 1581.

57.play: Most lamentable day, most woeful day

58.play: That ever, ever I did yet behold!

59.play: O day, O day, O day, O hateful day!

60.play: Never was seen so black a day as this!

61.play: O woeful day, O woeful day!

63.play: Most detestable death, by thee beguiled,

64.play: By cruel, cruel thee quite overthrown!

65.play: O love! O life! Not life, but love in death!

67.play: Uncomfortable time, why cam’st thou now

69.play: O child! O child! My soul and not my child!

70.play: Dead art thou! Alack, my child is dead,

71.play: And with my child my joys are burièd.

72.play: Peace, ho, for shame! Confusion’s cure lives not
notes: Peace, ho, for shame! / Peace, &c.] BIRCH (‘Philosophy and Religion of Sh.’). The Friar employs the language of religion equivocally, or gives a meaning to it in words, which, from the occasion, proves false. When Juliet is merely sleeping from the effects of a draught given to her by himself, he addresses the consolations of religion to her family as though she were dead. He calls the grief of her relatives on this occasion ‘reason’s merriment,’ and foregoes the character of a priest when she is really dead.
lives] DYCE (ed. 2). Here too LETTSOM would alter ‘lives’ to ‘lies’ (Live and lie, as we have already seen, were frequently confounded by transcribers and printers).

Peace ... merriment] CAMBR. Instead of this speech POPE has the following: ‘Fri. Oh peace for shame— Your daughter lives in peace and happiness, And it is vain to wish it otherwise. Heav’n and your self had part in this fair maid, Now heav’n hath all— Come stick your rosemary on this fair corpse, And as the custom of our country is, In all her best and sumptuous ornaments Convey her where her ancestors lie tomb’d.’ The last three lines are verbatim from (Q1.). HANMER follows POPE, with a different arrangement in the first lines, which he prints thus: ‘Oh peace for shame—your daughter lives in peace And happiness, and it is vain to wish It otherwise. Heav’n and your self had part In this fair maid, now heaven hath her all— Come’ &c.

73.play: In these confusions. Heaven and yourself

74.play: Had part in this fair maid. Now heaven hath all,

75.play: And all the better is it for the maid.

76.play: Your part in her you could not keep from death,

77.play: But heaven keeps his part in eternal life.

78.play: The most you sought was her promotion,

79.play: For ’twas your heaven she should be advanced;

80.play: And weep you now, seeing she is advanced

81.play: Above the clouds, as high as heaven itself?

82.play: O, in this love you love your child so ill

83.play: That you run mad, seeing that she is well.
notes: she is well] CLARKE. One of several allusions in Sh. to the conventional mode of saying of the dead that they are ‘well.’ See Wint. T., V, i, 30.

84.play: She’s not well married that lives married long,

85.play: But she’s best married that dies married young.

86.play: Dry up your tears, and stick your rosemary
notes: rosemary] DOUCE. This plant was used in various ways at funerals. Being an evergreen, it was regarded as an emblem of immortality. In an obituary kept by Mr. Smith, preserved in the British Museum, is the following: ‘Janv. 2, 1671. Mr. Cornelius Bee bookseller in Little Britain died; buried Jan. 4. at Great St. Bartholomew’s without a sermon, without wine or wafers, only gloves and rosemary.’ And Gay, when describing Blouzelinda’s funeral, records that ‘Sprigg’d rosemary the lads and lasses bore.’ NARES. It was carried at funerals, probably, for its odour, and as a token of remembrance of the deceased; noticed as late as the time of Gay, in his Pastoral Dirge DYCE. This plant was formerly supposed to strengthen the memory: ‘He from his lasse him launder hath sent, Shewing her loue, and doth requitall craue; Him rosemary his sweet-heart, whose intent Is that he her shouid in remembrance haue.’—Drayton’s Ninth Eglogue.

87.play: On this fair corse, and, as the custom is,
notes: as the custom is] HUNTER. ‘The burials are so strange both in Venice and all other cities, towns, and parishes of Italy, that they differ not only from England but from all other nations whatever in Christendom. For they carry the corse to church with face, hands, and feet all naked, and wearing the same apparel that the person wore lately before it died, or that which it craved to be buried in; which apparel

88.play: And in her best array, bear her to church,

89.play: For though fond nature bids us all lament,
notes: fond nature] KNT. The difficulty of some is not manifest. Some nature—some impulses of nature—some part of our nature. The idea may have suggested the ‘some natural tears’ of Milton.COLLI. (ed. 1). Some was of old written with a long ſ, which might be easily mistaken for an f, and frequently it was so mistaken. [Verp.DEL. Fond (i. e., foolish) nature stands in opposition to reason.DYCE. ‘Fond,’ whether the author's word or not, makes at least sense. ‘Some’ makes downright nonsense.

90.play: Yet nature’s tears are reason’s merriment.

91.play: All things that we ordainèd festival

92.play: Turn from their office to black funeral:

94.play: Our wedding cheer to a sad burial feast,
notes: burial feast] SING. It was anciently the custom to give an entertainment at a funeral. The usage was derived from the Roman cena funeralia, and is not yet disused in the North, where it is called an arval supper.

95.play: Our solemn hymns to sullen dirges change,

96.play: Our bridal flowers serve for a buried corse,

97.play: And all things change them to the contrary.

98.play: Sir, go you in, and, madam, go with him,

99.play: And go, Sir Paris. Everyone prepare

100.play: To follow this fair corse unto her grave.

101.play: The heavens do lour upon you for some ill.

102.play: Move them no more by crossing their high will. All but the Nurse and the Musicians exit.

103.play: Faith, we may put up our pipes and be gone.

104.play: Honest good fellows, ah, put up, put up,

105.play: For, well you know, this is a pitiful case.

106.play: Ay, by my troth, the case may be amended. Nurse exits.

notes: Enter Peter] CLARKE. [From the Qq we find that] William Kemp or Kempe originally played the part of Peter. We meet with the name of this actor

108.play: PETER: Musicians, O musicians, “Heart’s ease,” “Heart’s ease.” O, an you will have me live, play “Heart’s ease.”
notes: Musicians] COLERIDGE ('Lit. Rem.,' vol. ii, p. 157). As the audience know that Juliet is not dead, this scene is perhaps excusable. But it is a strong warning to minor dramatists not to introduce at one time many separate characters agitated by one and the same circumstance. It is difficult to understand what effect, whether that of pity or of laughter, Sh. meant to produce;—the occasion and the characteristic speeches are so little in harmony! For example, what the Nurse says is excellently suited to the Nurse's character, but grotesquely unsuited to the occasion. [Verp. Huds.] KNT. Rightly understood, this scene requires no apology. It was the custom of our ancient theatre to introduce, in the irregular pauses of a play that stood in place of a division into acts, some short diversion, such as a song, a dance, or the extempore buffoonery of a clown. At this point of Romeo and Juliet there is a natural pause in the action, and at this point such an interlude would, probably, have been presented, whether Sh. had written one or not. The stage-direction in Q2 puts this matter beyond a doubt. That direction says, ‘Enter Will Kempe,’ and the dialogue immediately begins between Peter and the musicians. Will Kempe was the Liston of his day, and was as great a popular favourite as Tarleton had been before him. It was wise, therefore, in Sh. to find some business for Will Kempe, that should not be entirely out of harmony with the great business of his play. This scene of the musicians is very short, and, regarded as a necessary part of the routine of the ancient stage, is excellently managed. Nothing can be more naturally exhibited than the indifference of hirelings, without attachment, to a family scene of grief. Peter and the musicians bandy jokes; and, although the musicians think Peter a ‘pestilent knave,’ perhaps for his inopportune sallies, they are ready enough to look after their own gratification, even amidst the sorrow which they see around them. A wedding or a burial is the same to them. ‘Come, we’ll in here—tarry for the mourners, and stay dinner.’ So Sh. read the course of the world—and it is not much changed. The quotation beginning, ‘When griping grief the heart doth wound,’ is from a short poem in The Paradise of Dainty Deuises, by Richard Edwards, master of the children of the chapel to Queen Elizabeth. This was set as a four-part song by Adrian Batten, organist of St. Paul's in the reign of Charles I, and is thus printed, but without any name, in Hawkins's History of Music, vol. v. The question of Peter, ‘Why silver sound, why music with her silver sound?’ is happily enough explained by Percy: ‘This ridicule is not so much levelled at the long itself (which, for the time it was written, is not inelegant) as at those forced and annual explanations often given by us painful editors and expositors of ancient

'Heart's ease'] COLL. (ed. 1). The name of a popular tune of the time. It is mentioned in ‘Misogonus,’ a MS. play by Thomas Rychardes, written before 1570 (see Hist. Eng. Dram. Poetry and the Stage, vol. ii, p. 470), where a song is sung to the tune. [Vere. Cham.DYCE (ed. 2). See Chappell's Popular Music of the Olden Time, &c., vol. i, p. 209 (ed. 2).

110.play: PETER: O musicians, because my heart itself plays “My heart is full.” O, play me some merry dump to comfort me.
notes: 'My heart is full of woe:'] STEEV. This is the burthen of the first stanza of A Pleasant new Ballad of Two Lovers: 'Hey hoe! my heart is full of woe.' [Sing. Hudds. Dyce.STA. It is in the Pepys collection, and begins thus: 'Complain, my lute, complaine on him, That stayes so long away; He promis'd to be here ere this, But still unkind doth stay; But now the proverbe true I finde, Once out of sight, then out of mind. Hey ho! my heart is full of woe.' [Cham.DYCE (ed. 2). The ballad just cited is of considerable merit, and the whole of it may be found in The Sh. Soc. Papers, vol. i, p. 12.

dump] STEEV. A dump anciently signified some kind of dance as well as sorrow. So in Humour Out of Breath, by John Day, 1607: 'He loves nothing but an Italian dump, Or a French brawl.' But here it means a mournful song.

111.play: FIRST MUSICIAN: Not a dump, we. ’Tis no time to play now.

114.play: PETER: I will then give it you soundly.

115.play: FIRST MUSICIAN: What will you give us?

116.play: PETER: No money, on my faith, but the gleek. I will give you the minstrel.
notes: gleek] STEEV. To gleek is to scoff, taken from an ancient game at cards called gleek. So in Turberville's translation of Ovid's Epistle from Dido to Æneas: ‘By manly mart to purchase prayse, And give his foes the gleeke.’ Again, in the argument to the same translator's version of Hermione to Orestes: ‘Orestes gave Achylles' sonne the gleeke.’ [Hal. RITSON. The use of this cant term is nowhere explained, and, in all probability, cannot, at this distance of time, be recovered. To gleek, however, signified to put a joke or trick upon a person, perhaps to jest according to the coarse humour of that age. [Hal. DOUCE. In some of the notes on this word it has been supposed to be connected with the card game of gleek; but it was not recollected that the Saxon language supplied the term Glig, ludibrium, and doubtless a corresponding verb. Thus glee signifies mirth, jocularity; and gleeman, or gligman, a minstrel or joculator. Gleek was, therefore, used to express a stronger sort of joke, a scoffing. It does not appear that the phrase to give the gleek was ever introduced in the above game, which was borrowed by us from the French, and derived from an original of very different import from the word in question. . . . To give the minstrel is no more than a punning phrase for giving the gleek. Minstrels and jesters were anciently called gleek-men or gligmen. [Dyce, Sing. Huds. Ital. NARES. To give the gleek meant to pass a jest upon, to make a person ridiculous. To give the minstrel, which follows, has no such meaning. Peter only means, ‘I will call you minstrel and so treat you,’ to which the musician replies, ‘Then I will give you the serving creature,’ as a personal retort in kind. [Sing. Dyce. STA. To give the gleek, a phrase borrowed from the old game of cards called gleek, signified to flout or scorn any one, and, as a gleekman or gligman was a name for a minstrel, we get a notion of the quibble meant. A similar equivoque is, no doubt, intended in ‘the serving creature,’ but the allusion is yet to be discovered. WHITE. The allusion to the glee-man or gligmon is obvious. Not so, however, the double meaning in the musician’s reply, unless Peter means that he will apply the term ‘minstrel’ reproachfully, and the musician that he will retort by calling Peter the servant to the minstrel.

the minstrel] STEEV. From the following entry on the books of the Stationer's Company, in the year 1560, it appears that the hire of a parson was cheaper than that of a minstrel or a cook. [See note on IV, ii, 2. ED.] [Hal.

117.play: FIRST MUSICIAN: Then will I give you the serving-creature.

118.play: PETER: Then will I lay the serving-creature’s dagger on your pate. I will carry no crochets. I’ll re you, I’ll fa you. Do you note me?
notes: dagger] CLARKE. Even in so slight a touch as this Sh. gives token of his sleepless attention to consistency and the production of dramatic verity in effect. Peter is thus shown to wear a knife or dagger about him, which he draws upon the slightest occasion of threat, whether made in joke or in earnest; and this serves to make more natural the point of Juliet’s wearing a dagger.

crotchets] ULR. A crotchet (so called because its shape is like that of a crook) is a quarter-note, and also a whim. Peter, therefore, intends to say, 'I will not endure your whims, your refusal to play,' but says, in effect, 'I will play no quarter-notes (but whole ones) on your pates.' CLARKE. An instance of Sh.'s using a familiarly known phrase, and varying it with one of his own introduced words. The effect is given of the then well-known phrase, 'I'll not carry coals,' meaning, 'I'll not put up with insults;' while, by introducing the word 'crotchets,' the joke is made doubly applicable to the rallying musician, in the sense of those musical symbols of notes denominated 'crotchets,' and those whimsies of banter sometimes jocosely so called.

re you, and fa you] KNT. Re and fa are the syllables or names given in solmization, or sol-faing, to the sounds D and F in the musical scale. [Verp. Sta. Ulr. ULR. 'To ray' also means 'to sift,' (sieben) and 'to fey' is 'to cleanse out' (schlämmen), both of which words are pronounced exactly like Re and Fa. Herein lies the wit of Peter. STA. The pun on note is self-evident, and the word appears to have been a favorite one to play upon, for Sh. has used it with a double meaning at least a score of times.

119.play: FIRST MUSICIAN: An you re us and fa us, you note us.

120.play: SECOND MUSICIAN: Pray you, put up your dagger and put out your wit.

121.play: PETER: Then have at you with my wit. I will dry-beat you with an iron wit, and put up my iron dagger. Answer me like men.
notes: Have . . . my wit] DEL. Beware of my wit. [Ulr.

122.play: [Sings.] When griping griefs the heart doth wound And doleful dumps the mind oppress, Then music with her silver sound— Why “silver sound”? Why “music with her silver sound”? What say you, Simon Catling?
notes: griping grief] STEEV. The epithet griping was by no means likely to excite laughter at the time it was written. Lord Surrey, in his second book of Virgil's Æneid, makes the hero say: 'New gripes of dred then pearse our trembling brestes.' [Clarke.] SIR JOHN HAWKINS. 'In Commendation of Musicke. 'Where griping grief ye ha t would wound, (and dolfull domps ye mind oppresse)

Catling] STEEV. A small lute-string made of catgut. [SING. HAL. A. C. In An Historical Account of Taxes under all Denominations in the Time of William and Mary, p. 336, is the following article: 'For every gross of catlings and lutestring,' &c. [HAL.

123.play: FIRST MUSICIAN: Marry, sir, because silver hath a sweet sound.

124.play: PETER: Prates.—What say you, Hugh Rebeck?
notes: Pretty!] DEL. Peter rejects the explanations of the musicians as 'babble.' By no means does he give his assent, as the reading Pretty adopted by the editors from (Q1) would represent. The omission of Thou before 'pratest' is not to be wondered at, and denotes the impertinent bearing with which Peter retorts upon the musicians. ULR. I have decided in favor of Q2, and take Prates as the plural of prate (gabble), believing that Pretty, even if ironical, accords but little with Peter's surly, gruff style; and that, on the other hand, 'gabble, babble, idiots,' or something similar, is the very answer that every one would expect from Peter's mouth. The plural, which is very remarkable, and which may have suggested to the compositor of Q3 (which follows F1) to put pratest, is readily explained, if it be assumed that

Kebeck] STEEV. An instrument with three strings, which is mentioned by several of the old writers. Rebeck, rebequina. See Menage, in v. Rebec. So in B. and Fl.'s Knight of the Burning Pestle: "'Tis present death for these fiddlers to tune their rebecks before the great Turk's grace." So in England's Helicon, 1600, is The Shepherd Arsilvus, his Song to his Rebeck, by Bar. Yong. [Hal. MAL. It is mentioned by Milton as an instrument of mirth: "When the merry bells ring round And the jocund rebecks sound." [Sing. Huds. Sta. Hal. NARES. An instrument of music, having catgut strings, and played with a bow, but originally with only two strings, then with three, till it was exalted into the more perfect violin, with four strings. It is thought to be the same with ribible, being a Moorish instrument, and in that language called rebeb. Thence it passed into Italy, where it became ribeca or ribeba, whence our English word. See Hawkins's Hist. of Music, II, p. 86. STA. It is frequently noticed by old writers: "He turned his rebeck to a mournful note."—Drayton, ed. II.

125.play: SECOND MUSICIAN: I say “silver sound” because musicians sound for silver.

126.play: PETER: Prates too.—What say you, James Soundpost?

127.play: THIRD MUSICIAN: Faith, I know not what to say.

128.play: PETER: O, I cry you mercy. You are the singer. I will say for you. It is “music with her silver sound” because musicians have no gold for sounding:
notes: musicians] STEEV. I should suspect that a fiddler made the alteration,—‘musicians.’ [Dyce. KNT. (ed. 2). It is interesting to mark the change in the corrected copy. Sh. would not put offensive words to the skilled in music, even into the mouth of a clownish servant.

129.play: [Sings.] Then music with her silver sound With speedy help doth lend redress. He exits.
notes: Exit] Dyce. Most editors print ‘Exit, singing;’ but surely Peter quotes the song without singing it.

130.play: FIRST MUSICIAN: What a pestilent knave is this same!

131.play: SECOND MUSICIAN: Hang him, Jack. Come, we’ll in here, tarry for the mourners, and stay dinner. They exit.
notes: Jack] Dyce. A common term of contempt and reproach (fellow, knave, rogue). [Clarke.

2.play: If I may trust the flattering truth of sleep,
notes: truth] Steev. If I may repose any confidence in the flattering visions of the night. [Sing. (ed. 1), Huds. Knt. It is not difficult to see the growth of that philosophical spirit in Sh. which suggested the substitution of the word ‘truth,’ which opens to the mind a deep volume of metaphysical inquiry. Coll. (ed. 1). ‘Flattering eye’ may be reconciled to sense, but with difficulty. Coll. (‘Notes and Emend.’) Nobody has been able at all satisfactorily to explain ‘flattering truth,’ since ‘truth’ cannot flatter; and Malone, not liking Johnson’s interpretation, preferred, what is to the full as unintelligible, the text of (Q₁). The real truth (not the ‘flattering truth’) seems to be that the old compositor was confounded between ‘trust’ in the first part of the line, and death near the end of it, and printed a word which he compounded of the beginning of the one word and of the end of the other. Sleep is often resembled to death, and death to sleep; and when Romeo,

death of sleep.] SING. (Sh. Vindicated). A more unhappy and absurd conjecture than this is scarcely to be paralleled, even by some of the other doings of the (MS.). I read ‘flattering soother sleep.’ The similarity of sound, in recitation, of the words truth of and soother, may have led to the error; and the poetical beauty of the passage is much heightened by the personification of sleep. DYCE (‘Few Notes). The meaning is, in vulgar prose,—If I may trust the visions with which my eye flattered me during sleep. I have not forgotten how our early writers characterize Sleep,—for instance, I recollect that Sleep is called by Sackville ‘cousin of Death’ and ‘a living death,’ and by Daniel, ‘brother to Death;’ but I remember nothing in the whole range of poetry which bears any resemblance to such a combination of words as ‘the flattering death of sleep’ of Collier’s (MS.); and, though I may lay myself open to the charge of presumption, I unhesitatingly assert, not only that the expression never could have come from Sh.’s pen, but that it is akin to nonsense. [Hal. DEL. That is, If I may trust that as true which sleep has revealed to me of a flattering nature. ULR. Romeo means to say, If I dare trust the truth which one is wont to impute to dreams, but which is only the truth of a flattery, therefore unsafe, untrustworthy, then my dreams presage, &c. I can find no meaning in the emendation of Collier’s (MS.). SING. (ed. 2). Sleep the poet elsewhere calls ‘balm of hurt minds,’ and ‘Nature’s soft nurse.’ STA. The ‘truth of sleep’ is even less intelligible than the ‘eye of sleep.’ By the latter Sh. perhaps meant vision, view, prospect. Thus in King John, II, i, 207: ‘These flags of France, that are advanced here Before the eye and prospect of your town.’ And in ‘Much Ado,’ IV, i, 228: ‘And every lovely organ of her life Shall come apparel’d in more precious habit, More moving—delicate and full of life, Into the eye and prospect of his soul.’

3.play: My dreams presage some joyful news at hand.

4.play: My bosom’s lord sits lightly in his throne,
notes: bosom’s lord] JOHNS. These three lines are very gay and pleasing. But why does Sh. give Romeo this involuntary cheerfulness just before the extremity of unhappiness? Perhaps to show the vanity of trusting to those uncertain and casual exaltations or depressions which many consider as certain fore-tokens of good or evil. [Sing. Corn. Verp. Huds. [See the notes on III, v, 53. ED.] STEEV. The poet has explained this passage himself a little further on, V, iii, 88. [Sing.] So in King Arthur, a Poem, by R. Chester, 1601: ‘How his deepe bosomes lord the dutchess thwarterd.’ The author, in a marginal note, declares that by bosom’s lord he means—Cupid. MAL. Thus, too, in Othello, III, iii, 448: ‘Yield up, O Love, thy crown and hearted throne.’ DEL. infers the same, from the same reference to Othello.
in his] WHITE. Here, as well as in the fifth line below, ‘in’ is used for ‘upon.

5.play: And all this day an unaccustomed spirit

6.play: Lifts me above the ground with cheerful thoughts.

7.play: I dreamt my lady came and found me dead

8.play: (Strange dream that gives a dead man leave to think!)

9.play: And breathed such life with kisses in my lips
notes: breathed such life] STEEV. Sh. seems here to have remembered Marlowe’s Hero and Leander, a poem, that he has quoted in As You Like It: ‘He kiss’d her and breath’d life into her lips,’ &c. [Sing.

10.play: That I revived and was an emperor.

11.play: Ah me, how sweet is love itself possessed

12.play: When but love’s shadows are so rich in joy!

13.play: [Enter Romeo’s man Balthasar, in riding boots.]

14.play: News from Verona!—How now, Balthasar?

15.play: Dost thou not bring me letters from the Friar?

16.play: How doth my lady? Is my father well?

17.play: How doth my Juliet? That I ask again,
notes: fares my Juliet] Coll. (ed. 1). The compositor, probably, caught the words, ‘How doth my lady,’ from the line immediately preceding, and thus injured [in QqFf] the rhythm of the passage. [Ulr. Del. A repetition of the question, almost word for word, is the more admissible here, since Romeo immediately adds, ‘That I ask again.’ White. ‘How doth my lady Juliet’ would clearly seem an accidental repetition of the question in the line immediately above it; even if it did not add two entirely superfluous syllables to the verse.

18.play: For nothing can be ill if she be well.

19.play: Then she is well and nothing can be ill.

20.play: Her body sleeps in Capels’ monument,
notes: Capels’] Mal. Sh. found Capel and Capulet used indiscriminately in Romeus and Juliet. [Sing. Del. This abbreviation is found only here and once afterwards in the same connection with monument. In (Q.) it occurs in other places.

21.play: And her immortal part with angels lives.

22.play: I saw her laid low in her kindred’s vault

23.play: And presently took post to tell it you.

24.play: O, pardon me for bringing these ill news,

25.play: Since you did leave it for my office, sir.

26.play: Is it e’en so?—Then I deny you, stars!—
notes: I defy you, stars] Ulr. ‘I deny you’ would at once turn Romeo into an atheist. Del. Romeo, in his death-defying despair, renounces the stars in which he had hitherto had faith. In King John I, i, 252, the phrase is used in the same sense: ‘As faithfully as I deny the devil.’ Coll. (ed. 2). Deny and ‘defy’ were, of old, used somewhat synonymously. It is ‘defy’ in the (MS.). White. Although the reading, ‘I deny you, stars,’ is not inappropriate, any doubts as to the presence in it of a slight typographical error are entirely removed by Romeo’s words in V, iii, 111. Clarke. There is a terribly quiet depth of concentrated anguish and will in this brief despairing ejaculation of Romeo’s that is more expressive than a hundred raving lines of lament would be. It is noteworthy, too, how the few pertinent words which follow are just to the point for dramatic purpose, and nothing more; while the servant’s observation, ‘Your looks are pale and wild,’ furnish significant comment.

27.play: Thou knowest my lodging. Get me ink and paper,

28.play: And hire post-horses. I will hence tonight.

29.play: I do beseech you, sir, have patience.

30.play: Your looks are pale and wild and do import

31.play: Some misadventure.

32.play: ROMEO: Tush, thou art deceived.

33.play: Leave me, and do the thing I bid thee do.

34.play: Hast thou no letters to me from the Friar?

35.play: No, my good lord.

36.play: ROMEO: No matter. Get thee gone,

37.play: And hire those horses. I’ll be with thee straight. Balthasar exits.

38.play: Well, Juliet, I will lie with thee tonight.

39.play: Let’s see for means. O mischief, thou art swift

40.play: To enter in the thoughts of desperate men.

41.play: I do remember an apothecary

42.play: (And hereabouts he dwells) which late I noted

43.play: In tattered weeds, with overwhelming brows,

44.play: Culling of simples. Meager were his looks.
notes: Culling of] ABBOTT (‘Shakespearian Grammar,’ P. 170, (ed. 3) 1870). Of naturally followed a verbal noun. In many cases we should call the verbal noun a participle, and the of has become unintelligible to us, because of the omission of the prepositional ‘a,’ ‘in,’ or ‘on.’ Thus ‘(a-)culling of,’ &c.

45.play: Sharp misery had worn him to the bones.
notes: Sharp misery] MAL. See Sackville’s description of Misery in his Induction: ‘His face was leane, and some deal pinde away; And eke his hands consumed to the bone.’ [Sing.

46.play: And in his needy shop a tortoise hung,

47.play: An alligator stuffed, and other skins
notes: An alligator stuff’d] MAL. It appears from Nashe’s Have With You to Saffron Walden, 1596, that a stuffed alligator, in Sh.’s time, made part of the furniture of an apothecary’s shop. ‘He made’ (says Nashe) ‘an anatomie of a rat, and after hanged her over his head, instead of an apothecary’s crocodile or dried alligator.’ [Sing. Hud.s. Sta. Clarke, Hal. STEEV. I was many years ago assured that, formerly, when an apothecary first engaged with his druggist, he was gratuitously furnished by him with these articles of show, which were then imported for that use only. I have met with the alligator, tortoise, &c., hanging up in the shop of an ancient apothecary at Limehouse, as well as in places more remote from our metropolis. See Hogarth, Marriage a la Mode, plate iii. It may be remarked, however, that the apothecaries dismissed their alligators, &c., some time before the physicians were willing to part with their amber-headed canes and solemn periwigs. [Sing. Hal. DOUCE. This word was probably introduced into the language by some of our early voyagers to the Spanish or Portuguese settlements in the newly-discovered world. They would hear the Spaniards discoursing of the animal by the name of el lagarto, or the lizard—Lat., lacerta; and on their return home they would inform their countrymen that this sort of crocodile was called an alligator. HALLIWELL. Mr. Fairholt sends me this note: ‘Romeo’s description of the shop of the poor apothecary may be accepted as minutely accurate, for it was customary with his class “to make a show,” according to their means. Rows of drug-bottles in Majolica, highly decorated by painting, filled their shelves, and are now among the most coveted articles to collectors of “Raffaelle-ware.” The apothecary’s shop was then (as it is now in Italy) the rendezvous for idlers and elderly gossips; hence the proprietor made the best display he could of his own position. Dried fishes and marine monsters were suspended from the ceiling; “an alligator stuff’d” was the most coveted and indispensable of all; and we rarely meet with any representation of the shop of the humblest medical practitioner without one. In Dutch art they abound. Our cut represents that of a village barber-surgeon after one of Teniers best pictures.’

48.play: Of ill-shaped fishes; and about his shelves,

49.play: A beggarly account of empty boxes,
notes: empty boxes] STEEV. This circumstance is likewise found in Painter, tom. ii, p. 241: ' ——— beholdyng an apoticaries shoppe of lytle furniture, and lesse store of boxes and other thynges requisite for that science, thought that the verie poverte of the mayster apothecarye would make him wyllngly yelde to that whych he pretended to demaunde.' [ITAL. MAL. It is clear, I think, that Sh. had here Brooke's poem before him: 'And secking long (alac, too soone), the thing he sought, he founde. An apothecary sate un'usied at his doore, Whom by his heavy countenance he gessed to be poore; And in his shop he saw his boxes were but fewe, And in his window (of his wares) there was so small a shew; Wherefore our Romeus assuredly hath thought, What by no frendship could be got, with money should be bought; For nedy lacke is lyke the poore man to compell To sell that which the cites lawe forbiddeth him to sell.— Take füte crownes of gold (quoth he) I geve them thee.— Fayre syr (quoth he), be sure this is the speedi? gere, And more there is then you shall nede; for halfe of that is there Wlll serve, I undertake, in lesse than halfe an howre, To kill the strongest man alive; such i« the poysons power.' [HAL. Cham.

50.play: Green earthen pots, bladders, and musty seeds,
notes: Green earthen pots] HALLIWELL. The manufacture of green earthen pots was carried on in England in Sh.'s time, as appears from the following curious letter, written in August, 1594, from Sir Julius Cæsar to Sir William Moore: 'After my hartie comendacions, &c., Wheras in tymes past the bearer hereof hath had out of the parke of Farnham, belonging to the Bishopprick of Winchester, certaine white clay for the making of grene potts usually drunk in by the gentlemen of the Temple; and nowe understandinge of some restraint thereof, and that you (amongst others) are authorized there in divers respects during the vacancye of the said Bushoppricke; my request therefore unto you is, and the rather for that I am a member of the said house, that you would in favour of us all permytt the bearer hereof to d’gge and carie awaye so muche of the said claye as by him shalbe thought sufficient for the furnishinge of the said house with grene potts as aforesaid, paying as he hath heretofore for the same. In accomplishement whereof, myself, with the whole societie, shall acknowledge ourselves muche beholden unto you, and shalbe readie to requite you, at all tymes hereafter, with the like pleasure. And so I bid you moste hartelie farewell.'

51.play: Remnants of packthread, and old cakes of roses

52.play: Were thinly scattered to make up a show.

53.play: Noting this penury, to myself I said

54.play: “An if a man did need a poison now,
notes: An if] ABBOTT (Shakespearian Grammar, 1869, Art. 37). This particle [An=if] has been derived from an, the imperative of anan, to grant. But the word is generally written and in Early English (Stratmann), and frequently in Elizabethan authors. . . . The true explanation appears to be that the hypothesis, the if, is expressed not by the and, but by the subjunctive, and that and merely means with the addition of, plus, just as but means leaving out, or minus. . . . Latterly, the subjunctive, falling into disuse, was felt to be too weak unaided to express the hypothesis; and the same tendency which introduced ‘more better,’ ‘most unkindest,’ &c., superseded and by and if, an if and if. There is nothing remarkable in the change of and into an. And, even in its ordinary sense, is often written an in Early English. (See Halliwell.)

55.play: Whose sale is present death in Mantua,
notes: Mantua] KNT. Sir Walter Raleigh, in his ‘Discourse of Tenures,’ says: ‘By the laws of Spain and Portugal it is not lawful to sell poison.’ A similar law, if we are rightly informed, prevailed in Italy. There is no such law in our own statute-book; and the circumstance is a remarkable exemplification of the difference between English and continental manners.

56.play: Here lives a caitiff wretch would sell it him.”

57.play: O, this same thought did but forerun my need,

58.play: And this same needy man must sell it me.

59.play: As I remember, this should be the house.

60.play: Being holiday, the beggar’s shop is shut.—

notes: What ho! apothecary] KNT. [gives the text of (Q1), and adds]: The studies in poetical art, which Sh.’s corrections of himself supply, are amongst the most instructive in the whole compass of literature. [Verp.

64.play: Come hither, man. I see that thou art poor. He offers money.

65.play: Hold, there is forty ducats. Let me have

66.play: A dram of poison, such soon-speeding gear

67.play: As will disperse itself through all the veins,

68.play: That the life-weary taker may fall dead,

69.play: And that the trunk may be discharged of breath

70.play: As violently as hasty powder fired

71.play: Doth hurry from the fatal cannon’s womb.

72.play: Such mortal drugs I have, but Mantua’s law

73.play: Is death to any he that utters them.

74.play: Art thou so bare and full of wretchedness,

75.play: And fearest to die? Famine is in thy cheeks,

76.play: Need and oppression starveth in thy eyes,

77.play: Contempt and beggary hangs upon thy back.
notes: upon thy back] STEEV. I have restored the reading of (Q₁) in preference to the line which is found in all the subsequent impressions. KNT. Steevens again! who has ‘recovered’ from (Q₁) the line in our common texts. STA. The reading of (Q₁) has at least equal force of expression.

78.play: The world is not thy friend, nor the world’s law.

79.play: The world affords no law to make thee rich.

80.play: Then be not poor, but break it, and take this.

81.play: My poverty, but not my will, consents.

82.play: I pay thy poverty and not thy will.
notes: I pay] DYCE (‘Few Notes,’ &c.). A writer in The Westminster Review, vol. xliv, p. 61, says that ‘Knight very properly restores the reading of Q₂ and F₁, “pray;” the relation here is between Romeo’s earnestly repeated prayer and the apothecary’s consent: the moment for paying him is not yet arrived.’ But what does the writer understand by the concluding words of Romeo’s preceding speech, ‘take this’? can he doubt that ‘this’ means the gold which Romeo holds in his hand ready to pay the Apothecary? WHITE. I pray is a palpable corruption. Romeo does not pray; but he does pay.

84.play: Put this in any liquid thing you will
notes: Put this] STEEV. Perhaps when Sh. allotted this speech to the Apothecary he had not quite forgot the following passage in The Pardoner’s Tale of Chaucer, 12794:

85.play: And drink it off, and if you had the strength

86.play: Of twenty men, it would dispatch you straight.

88.play: There is thy gold, worse poison to men’s souls,

89.play: Doing more murder in this loathsome world

90.play: Than these poor compounds that thou mayst not sell.

91.play: I sell thee poison; thou hast sold me none.

92.play: Farewell, buy food, and get thyself in flesh. Apothecary exits.

93.play: Come, cordial and not poison, go with me

94.play: To Juliet’s grave, for there must I use thee. He exits.

2.play: Holy Franciscan friar, brother, ho!

3.play: [Enter Friar Lawrence.]

4.play: This same should be the voice of Friar John.—

5.play: Welcome from Mantua. What says Romeo?

6.play: Or, if his mind be writ, give me his letter.

7.play: Going to find a barefoot brother out,
notes: Going to find] KNT. Friar Laurence and his associates must be supposed to belong to the Franciscan ord. of friars. In his kindliness, his learning, and his inclination to mix with and, perhaps, control the affairs of the world, he is no unapt representative of one of this distinguished order in their best days. Warton, in his History of English Poetry, has described the learning, the magnificence, and the prodigious influence of this remarkable body. Friar Laurence was able to give to Romeo ‘Adversity’s sweet milk—philosophy.’ He was to Romeo ‘a divine, a ghostly confessor, A sin-absolver, and my friend professed;’ but he was yet of the world. He married Romeo and his mistress, partly to gratify their love, and partly to secure his influence in the reconciliation of their families. Warton says the Franciscans ‘managed the machines of every important operation or event, both in the religious and political world.’ MAL. So in Romeus and Juliet:Apace our frier John to Mantua him hyes;And, for because in Italy it is a wonted gyseThat friers in the towne should seeldome walke alone,But of theyr covent ay should be accompanièd with one Of his professyon, straight a house he fyndeth out,In mynde to take some frier with him, to walke the towne about.’ Our author, having occasion for Friar John, has here departed from the poem, and supposed the pestilence to rage at Verona, instead of Mantua. [Sing. Huds. Kn.]

8.play: One of our order, to associate me,
notes: to associate] STEEV. Each friar has always a companion assigned him by the Superior when he asks leave to go out; and thus, says Baretti, they are a check upon each other. [Sing. Corn. Verp. Huds. White (substantially), Cham. Hal.] HOLT WHITE. In the Visitatio Notabilis de Seleburne, a curious record printed in The Natural History and Antiquities of Selborne, Wykeham enjoins the canons not to go abroad without leave from the prior, who is ordered on such occasions to assign the brother a companion, ne suspicio sinistra vel scandalum oriatur.—Append., p. 448. [Sing. Huds. Sta. Hal.]

9.play: Here in this city visiting the sick,

10.play: And finding him, the searchers of the town,

11.play: Suspecting that we both were in a house

12.play: Where the infectious pestilence did reign,

13.play: Sealed up the doors and would not let us forth,

14.play: So that my speed to Mantua there was stayed.

15.play: Who bare my letter, then, to Romeo?

16.play: I could not send it—here it is again— Returning the letter.

17.play: Nor get a messenger to bring it thee,

18.play: So fearful were they of infection.

19.play: Unhappy fortune! By my brotherhood,

20.play: The letter was not nice but full of charge,

21.play: Of dear import, and the neglecting it

22.play: May do much danger. Friar John, go hence.

23.play: Get me an iron crow and bring it straight

24.play: Unto my cell.

25.play: Brother, I’ll go and bring it thee.   He exits.

26.play: Now must I to the monument alone.

27.play: Within this three hours will fair Juliet wake.

28.play: She will beshrew me much that Romeo

29.play: Hath had no notice of these accidents.

30.play: But I will write again to Mantua,

31.play: And keep her at my cell till Romeo come.

32.play: Poor living corse, closed in a dead man’s tomb! He exits.

2.play: Give me thy torch, boy. Hence and stand aloof.

3.play: Yet put it out, for I would not be seen.

4.play: Under yond yew trees lay thee all along,
notes: yond yew-trees] COLL. (ed. 1). Balthasar afterwards speaks of a ‘young tree’ in the churchyard, but probably we ought again to read yew-tree. Sh. would hardly have written yond’ young.

5.play: Holding thy ear close to the hollow ground.

6.play: So shall no foot upon the churchyard tread

7.play: (Being loose, unfirm, with digging up of graves)

8.play: But thou shalt hear it. Whistle then to me

9.play: As signal that thou hearest something approach.

10.play: Give me those flowers. Do as I bid thee. Go.

11.play: PAGE, aside

12.play: I am almost afraid to stand alone

13.play: Here in the churchyard. Yet I will adventure. He moves away from Paris.

14.play: PARIS, scattering flowers

15.play: Sweet flower, with flowers thy bridal bed I strew

16.play: (O woe, thy canopy is dust and stones!)

17.play: Which with sweet water nightly I will dew,

18.play: Or, wanting that, with tears distilled by moans.

19.play: The obsequies that I for thee will keep

20.play: Nightly shall be to strew thy grave and weep. Page whistles.

21.play: The boy gives warning something doth approach.

22.play: What cursèd foot wanders this way tonight,

23.play: To cross my obsequies and true love’s rite?
notes: rite] DEL. The reading of (Q1) fails to convey the meaning.

24.play: What, with a torch? Muffle me, night, awhile. He steps aside.
notes: muffle] STEEV. Thus in Drayton's Polyolbion: 'But suddenly the clouds, which on the winds do fly, Do muffle him againe.' Muffle was not become a low ['unpoetical,' SING. (ed. 1)] word even in the time of Milton, as the Elder Brother in Comus uses it: 'Unmuffle, ye faint stars,' &c. A muffler was a part of female dress. [SING. DYCE. A muffler is a sort of wrapper worn by women, which generally covered the mouth and chin, but sometimes almost the whole face.

25.play: [Enter Romeo and Balthasar.]
notes: Balthasar] COLL. Possibly Kemp doubled his part, and acted both Peter and Balthasar, as both were short, and hence the confusion. [ULR. DEL. WHITE.

26.play: Give me that mattock and the wrenching iron.

27.play: Hold, take this letter. Early in the morning

28.play: See thou deliver it to my lord and father.

29.play: Give me the light. Upon thy life I charge thee,

30.play: Whate’er thou hearest or seest, stand all aloof

31.play: And do not interrupt me in my course.

32.play: Why I descend into this bed of death

33.play: Is partly to behold my lady’s face,

34.play: But chiefly to take thence from her dead finger

35.play: A precious ring, a ring that I must use

36.play: In dear employment. Therefore hence, begone.
notes: dear employment] JOHNSON. That is, action of importance. [Sing. STEEV. Ben Jonson uses the word dear in the same sense in Catiline, Act I: 'Put your known talents on so dear a business.' [Sing. SINGER [Note on Twelfth Night, V, i, 74: 'in terms so bloody and so dear']. Tooke has so admirably accounted for the epithet dear applied by our ancient writers to any object which excites a sensation of hurt, pain, and consequently of anxiety, solicitude, care, earnestness, that I shall extract it as the best comment upon the apparently opposite uses of the word in our great poet: 'Dearth is the third person singular of the English (from the Anglo-Saxon verb Derian, nocere, lædere), to dere. It means some or any season, weather, or other cause, which dereth, i. e., maketh dear, hurteth, or doth mischief. The English verb to dere was formerly in common use.'
